infobox.value: Liberty Square; [Lužánky](/wiki/Lu%C5%BE%C3%A1nky "Lužánky") Park; *[Ignis Brunensis](/wiki/Ignis_Brunensis "Ignis Brunensis")*; [Špilberk Castle](/wiki/%C5%A0pilberk_Castle "Špilberk Castle"); [Brno Exhibition Centre](/wiki/Brno_Exhibition_Centre "Brno Exhibition Centre"); [Villa Tugendhat](/wiki/Villa_Tugendhat "Villa Tugendhat"); and [Cathedral of St. Peter and Paul](/wiki/Cathedral_of_St._Peter_and_Paul,_Brno "Cathedral of St. Peter and Paul, Brno") | |

([/ˈbɜːrnoʊ/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*BUR-noh*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"),[[5]](#cite_note-5) Czech pronunciation: [[ˈbr̩no]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Czech "Help:IPA/Czech") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Cs-Brno.ogg "File:Cs-Brno.ogg"); [German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Brünn* [[bʁʏn]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:De-Br%C3%BCnn.ogg "File:De-Brünn.ogg")) is a [city](/wiki/Statutory_city_(Czech_Republic) "Statutory city (Czech Republic)") in the [South Moravian Region](/wiki/South_Moravian_Region "South Moravian Region") of the [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic "Czech Republic"). Located at the confluence of the [Svitava](/wiki/Svitava_(river) "Svitava (river)") and [Svratka](/wiki/Svratka_(river) "Svratka (river)") rivers, Brno has about 403,000 inhabitants, making it the second-largest city in the Czech Republic after the capital, [Prague](/wiki/Prague "Prague"), and one of the [100 largest cities in the European Union](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_the_European_Union_by_population_within_city_limits "List of cities in the European Union by population within city limits"). The [Brno metropolitan area](/wiki/Brno_metropolitan_area "Brno metropolitan area") has approximately 730,000 inhabitants.

in German, ברין (**Brin**) in [Yiddish](/wiki/Yiddish "Yiddish"), and **Bruna** in [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin"). The town was also referred to as Brunn ([/brʌn/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"))[[27]](#cite_note-27) in English, but this variant is no longer common today.[[28]](#cite_note-28) The asteroid [2889 Brno](/wiki/2889_Brno "2889 Brno") was named after the city, as was the [Bren light machine gun](/wiki/Bren_light_machine_gun "Bren light machine gun"). **Brno** ([/ˈbɜːrnoʊ/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*BUR-noh*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"),[[5]](#cite_note-5) Czech pronunciation: [[ˈbr̩no]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Czech "Help:IPA/Czech") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Cs-Brno.ogg "File:Cs-Brno.ogg"); [German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Brünn* [[bʁʏn]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:De-Br%C3%BCnn.ogg "File:De-Brünn.ogg")) is a [city](/wiki/Statutory_city_(Czech_Republic) "Statutory city (Czech Republic)") in the [South Moravian Region](/wiki/South_Moravian_Region "South Moravian Region") of the [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic "Czech Republic"). Located at the confluence of the [Svitava](/wiki/Svitava_(river) "Svitava (river)") and [Svratka](/wiki/Svratka_(river) "Svratka (river)") rivers, Brno has about 403,000 inhabitants, making it the second-largest city in the Czech Republic after the capital, [Prague](/wiki/Prague "Prague"), and one of the [100 largest cities in the European Union](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_the_European_Union_by_population_within_city_limits "List of cities in the European Union by population within city limits"). The [Brno metropolitan area](/wiki/Brno_metropolitan_area "Brno metropolitan area") has approximately 730,000 inhabitants.

Brno served as the capital of [Moravia](/wiki/Moravia "Moravia") from the Middle Ages until 1948, and remains the political and cultural hub of the South Moravian Region. Brno is an important centre of the [Czech judiciary](/wiki/Judiciary_of_the_Czech_Republic "Judiciary of the Czech Republic"). The [Constitutional Court](/wiki/Constitutional_Court_of_the_Czech_Republic "Constitutional Court of the Czech Republic"), the [Supreme Court](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_Czech_Republic "Supreme Court of the Czech Republic"), the [Supreme Administrative Court](/wiki/Supreme_Administrative_Court_of_the_Czech_Republic "Supreme Administrative Court of the Czech Republic"), the Supreme Public Prosecutor's Office, as well as state authorities, such as the [Ombudsman](/wiki/Ombudsman "Ombudsman")[[6]](#cite_note-pdr-6) and the Office for the Protection of Competition, are all located here.[[7]](#cite_note-7) Brno is also an important centre of learning and higher education, with 10 universities, 29 faculties and a student population of over 65,000, as well as more than 60 secondary schools throughout the city.[[8]](#cite_note-:7-8)[[9]](#cite_note-:6-9)[[10]](#cite_note-:8-10)

The [Brno Exhibition Centre](/wiki/Brno_Exhibition_Centre "Brno Exhibition Centre") is one of the largest in Europe.[[11]](#cite_note-bec-11) The complex opened in 1928 and has a long history of hosting international [trade fairs](/wiki/Trade_fair "Trade fair") and expositions.[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13) The [Masaryk Circuit](/wiki/Masaryk_Circuit "Masaryk Circuit") has been hosting motorsport events since 1930, including the [Road Racing World Championship Grand Prix](/wiki/Road_Racing_World_Championship_Grand_Prix "Road Racing World Championship Grand Prix").[[14]](#cite_note-brnocircuit-14) Another local tradition is the international fireworks competition and drone show *[Ignis Brunensis](/wiki/Ignis_Brunensis "Ignis Brunensis")*, which attracts over a million visitors annually.[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-:9-16)

Brno's traditional symbols are two medieval landmarks which dominate the cityscape: the historic [Špilberk Castle](/wiki/%C5%A0pilberk_Castle "Špilberk Castle") and its fortifications, and the [Cathedral of Saints Peter and Paul](/wiki/Cathedral_of_St._Peter_and_Paul,_Brno "Cathedral of St. Peter and Paul, Brno") on Petrov Hill.[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18) Another historic landmark is the [Veveří Castle](/wiki/Veve%C5%99%C3%AD_Castle "Veveří Castle") near the [Brno Reservoir](/wiki/Brno_Reservoir "Brno Reservoir").[[19]](#cite_note-veveri-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-brnoinfo-21) The [Villa Tugendhat](/wiki/Villa_Tugendhat "Villa Tugendhat"), a seminal example of [functionalist architecture](/wiki/Functionalism_(architecture) "Functionalism (architecture)"), was added to the [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") [World Heritage Sites List](/wiki/List_of_World_Heritage_Sites_by_year_of_inscription "List of World Heritage Sites by year of inscription") in 2001.[[22]](#cite_note-22) One of the natural sights outside the city is the [Moravian Karst](/wiki/Moravian_Karst "Moravian Karst"). Brno is a member of the [UNESCO Creative Cities Network](/wiki/Creative_Cities_Network "Creative Cities Network") and was designated a "[City of Music](/wiki/City_of_Music_(UNESCO) "City of Music (UNESCO)")" in 2017.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

## Names

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brno&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Names")]

The [etymology](/wiki/Etymology "Etymology") of the name Brno is disputed. It might be derived from [Old Czech](/wiki/History_of_the_Czech_language "History of the Czech language") **brnie**, i.e. 'muddy', 'swampy'.[[26]](#cite_note-26) Alternatives are the [Slavic](/wiki/Slavic_languages "Slavic languages") verb *brniti* ('to armour' or 'to fortify') or a [Celtic](/wiki/Celtic_languages "Celtic languages") origin. The latter theory would make it [cognate](/wiki/Cognate "Cognate") with other Celtic words for hill, such as the [Welsh](/wiki/Welsh_language "Welsh language") word **bryn**. Throughout its history, locals in Brno have also referred to their city in different languages, including **Brünn** in German, ברין (**Brin**) in [Yiddish](/wiki/Yiddish "Yiddish"), and **Bruna** in [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin"). The town was also referred to as Brunn ([/brʌn/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"))[[27]](#cite_note-27) in English, but this variant is no longer common today.[[28]](#cite_note-28) The asteroid [2889 Brno](/wiki/2889_Brno "2889 Brno") was named after the city, as was the [Bren light machine gun](/wiki/Bren_light_machine_gun "Bren light machine gun"). The latter was in wide use during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), and its name derives from 'Brno' and '[Enfield](/wiki/London_Borough_of_Enfield "London Borough of Enfield")', the site of the [Royal Small Arms Factory](/wiki/Royal_Small_Arms_Factory "Royal Small Arms Factory").[[29]](#cite_note-29)[[30]](#cite_note-Burian-30)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brno&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: History")]

Main articles: [History of Brno](/wiki/History_of_Brno "History of Brno") and [Timeline of Brno](/wiki/Timeline_of_Brno "Timeline of Brno")

### Early history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brno&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Early history")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/Mince_10K%C4%8D_vzor_2003_rubov%C3%A1_strana.jpg/250px-Mince_10K%C4%8D_vzor_2003_rubov%C3%A1_strana.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mince_10K%C4%8D_vzor_2003_rubov%C3%A1_strana.jpg)

The 10 [CZK](/wiki/Czech_koruna "Czech koruna") coin (1993 design)

The Brno basin has been inhabited since [prehistoric times](/wiki/Prehistoric_Europe "Prehistoric Europe"), as evidenced by a 2024 discovery of mammoth bones and prehistoric tools dating back 15,000 years.[[31]](#cite_note-31) [Dolní Věstonice](/wiki/Doln%C3%AD_V%C4%9Bstonice "Dolní Věstonice"), a globally significant [Paleolithic](/wiki/Paleolithic "Paleolithic") site, is located 30 km (19 mi) south of Brno.[[32]](#cite_note-32) Traces of Neanderthal activity were found at *[Švédův stůl](/w/index.php?title=%C5%A0v%C3%A9d%C5%AFv_st%C5%AFl&action=edit&redlink=1 "Švédův stůl (page does not exist)") [[cs](https://cs.wikipedia.org/wiki/%C5%A0v%C3%A9d%C5%AFv_st%C5%AFl "cs:Švédův stůl")]* in the Moravian Karst, 10 km (6.2 mi) to the northeast.[[33]](#cite_note-:11-33)[[34]](#cite_note-34) The city's direct precursor was a fortified settlement, *[Staré Zámky](/w/index.php?title=Star%C3%A9_Z%C3%A1mky&action=edit&redlink=1 "Staré Zámky (page does not exist)") [[cs](https://cs.wikipedia.org/wiki/Star%C3%A9_Z%C3%A1mky "cs:Staré Zámky")],* established during the [Great Moravian Empire](/wiki/Great_Moravia "Great Moravia"), which was inhabited from the [Neolithic Age](/wiki/Neolithic_Age "Neolithic Age") until the early 11th century.[[35]](#cite_note-35) The site of the Staré Zámky Hillfort is located 1.5 km (0.93 mi) northeast of [Brno-Líšeň](/wiki/Brno-L%C3%AD%C5%A1e%C5%88 "Brno-Líšeň"). During the Great Moravian period, the Hillfort played a crucial role as a strategic hub, connecting the empire's centre with the ironworks in the [Moravian Karst](/wiki/Moravian_Karst "Moravian Karst"). Around the turn of the 9th and 10th centuries, the Hillfort experienced a catastrophic fire. Its remnants are protected as an archaeological monument.[[36]](#cite_note-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37)

### Middle Ages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brno&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Middle Ages")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/Znak_Moravsk%C3%A9ho_markrabstv%C3%AD.png/250px-Znak_Moravsk%C3%A9ho_markrabstv%C3%AD.png)](/wiki/File:Znak_Moravsk%C3%A9ho_markrabstv%C3%AD.png)

Coat of arms of the margraviate

In the early 11th century, Brno was established as the seat of a non-ruling prince from the [House of Přemyslid](/wiki/House_of_P%C5%99emyslid "House of Přemyslid"),[[38]](#cite_note-brnohist-38) and it became one of the centres of Moravia, along with [Olomouc](/wiki/Olomouc "Olomouc") and [Znojmo](/wiki/Znojmo "Znojmo"). Brno was first mentioned in Cosmas' *[Chronica Boemorum](/wiki/Chronica_Boemorum "Chronica Boemorum"),* dating to 1091, when King [Vratislaus II](/wiki/Vratislaus_II_of_Bohemia "Vratislaus II of Bohemia") besieged his brother, [Conrad](/wiki/Conrad_I,_Duke_of_Bohemia "Conrad I, Duke of Bohemia"), at Brno castle.[[39]](#cite_note-39) In the mid-11th century, Moravia was divided into three separate territories. Each had its own ruler, who came from the [Přemyslids](/wiki/P%C5%99emyslids "Přemyslids") dynasty but was independent of the other two, and subordinate to the [Bohemian](/wiki/Bohemia "Bohemia") ruler in [Prague](/wiki/Prague "Prague"). The seats of these rulers and thus the "capitals" of these three territories were the castles and towns of Brno, [Olomouc](/wiki/Olomouc "Olomouc"), and [Znojmo](/wiki/Znojmo "Znojmo"). In the late 12th century, Moravia began to reunify, forming the [Margraviate of Moravia](/wiki/Margraviate_of_Moravia "Margraviate of Moravia"). From then until the middle of the 17th century, political power was divided between Brno and Olomouc, with Znojmo playing a tertiary role. Olomouc had a larger population than Brno and was also the seat of the [Diocese of Olomouc](/wiki/Archdiocese_of_Olomouc "Archdiocese of Olomouc"), the sole bishopric in Moravia until 1777.[[40]](#cite_note-40)[[41]](#cite_note-gemaart-41) The [Moravian Diet](/wiki/Moravian_Diet "Moravian Diet"), the [Moravian Land Tables](/w/index.php?title=Moravian_Land_Tables&action=edit&redlink=1 "Moravian Land Tables (page does not exist)") [[cs](https://cs.wikipedia.org/wiki/Moravsk%C3%A9_zemsk%C3%A9_desky "cs:Moravské zemské desky")], and the [Moravian Land Court](/w/index.php?title=Moravian_Land_Court&action=edit&redlink=1 "Moravian Land Court (page does not exist)") [[cs](https://cs.wikipedia.org/wiki/Moravsk%C3%BD_zemsk%C3%BD_soud "cs:Moravský zemský soud")] convened in both cities.[[42]](#cite_note-42)[[43]](#cite_note-43) These assemblies made political, legal, and financial decisions.[[38]](#cite_note-brnohist-38)

Before the 13th century, there were virtually no Germans living in the Bohemian Lands. Bohemia and Moravia-Silesia were inhabited by an essentially all-Slavic population.[[44]](#cite_note-:5-44) In 1243, the separate settlements that made up Brno were consolidated into a single fortified settlement and granted a royal city charter by King [Wenceslaus I](/wiki/Wenceslaus_I_of_Bohemia "Wenceslaus I of Bohemia") of Bohemia.[[45]](#cite_note-45)[[46]](#cite_note-46)[[47]](#cite_note-47)[[48]](#cite_note-48) As was common throughout Central and Eastern Europe at the time, the royal city charter was closely tied to [the arrival of German settlers](/wiki/Ostsiedlung "Ostsiedlung") and other migrants.[[49]](#cite_note-49)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0f/Veduta_z_obl%C3%A9h%C3%A1n%C3%AD_Brna_%C5%A0v%C3%A9dy_v_roce_1645.jpg/250px-Veduta_z_obl%C3%A9h%C3%A1n%C3%AD_Brna_%C5%A0v%C3%A9dy_v_roce_1645.jpg)](/wiki/File:Veduta_z_obl%C3%A9h%C3%A1n%C3%AD_Brna_%C5%A0v%C3%A9dy_v_roce_1645.jpg)

Unsuccessful Swedish siege in 1645

Around the turn of the 13th century, merchants on the main market, now known as [Zelný trh](/wiki/Zeln%C3%BD_trh "Zelný trh"), one of Brno's oldest squares, began building cellars to store food, wine, and beer. Although these vaults initially supported only local commerce, they became a vital part of the city's infrastructure and served as temporary shelters in wartime. They expanded over time, particularly in the Baroque period, and are now known as Labyrinth Under the Vegetable Market (Czech: *Labyrint pod Zelným trhem*).[[50]](#cite_note-50)[[51]](#cite_note-51) In 1324, Queen [Elisabeth Richeza of Poland](/wiki/Elisabeth_Richeza_of_Poland "Elisabeth Richeza of Poland") built the [Basilica of the Assumption of Our Lady](/wiki/Basilica_of_the_Assumption_of_Our_Lady,_Brno "Basilica of the Assumption of Our Lady, Brno"), where she is now buried, along with a [Cistercian convent](/wiki/Cistercian_Convent "Cistercian Convent"), which later became an [Augustinian Abbey](/wiki/St_Thomas%27s_Abbey,_Brno "St Thomas's Abbey, Brno").[[52]](#cite_note-52)

From the mid-14th century to the early 15th century, during the rule of the Moravian branch of the Luxembourg dynasty, Špilberk Castle served as the official seat of the Moravian margraves. One of them, [Jobst of Moravia](/wiki/Jobst_of_Moravia "Jobst of Moravia"), was elected the [King of the Romans](/wiki/King_of_the_Romans "King of the Romans").[[53]](#cite_note-53)[[54]](#cite_note-54) The Margraviate of Moravia was reunited under the Bohemian crown after his death.[[55]](#cite_note-55) During the [Hussite Wars](/wiki/Hussite_Wars "Hussite Wars"), Brno was besieged by the [Hussites](/wiki/Hussites "Hussites") twice, in 1428 and in 1430. Both attempts to conquer the city were unsuccessful.

### 17th and 18th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brno&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 17th and 18th century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cd/Veduta_Brna_se_znakem_Markrabstv%C3%AD_moravsk%C3%A9ho%2C_rok_1670.jpg/250px-Veduta_Brna_se_znakem_Markrabstv%C3%AD_moravsk%C3%A9ho%2C_rok_1670.jpg)](/wiki/File:Veduta_Brna_se_znakem_Markrabstv%C3%AD_moravsk%C3%A9ho,_rok_1670.jpg)

Coat of arms of the Margraviate of Moravia in *Book of the state of lords* with the picture of Brno (1670)

In 1641, during the [Thirty Years' War](/wiki/Thirty_Years%27_War "Thirty Years' War"), the Holy Roman Emperor and the margrave of Moravia, [Ferdinand III](/wiki/Ferdinand_III,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Ferdinand III, Holy Roman Emperor"), ordered the permanent relocation of the Diet, the Land Court, and the Land Tables from Olomouc to Brno, as [Collegium Nordicum](/wiki/Collegium_Nordicum "Collegium Nordicum") had made Olomouc the primary target of the Swedish armies.[[56]](#cite_note-Garstein-56) In 1642, Olomouc surrendered to the [Swedish Army](/wiki/Military_of_the_Swedish_Empire "Military of the Swedish Empire"), which occupied it for eight years.[[a]](#cite_note-57) Brno, under the leadership of [Jean-Louis Raduit de Souches](/wiki/Jean-Louis_Raduit_de_Souches "Jean-Louis Raduit de Souches"), successfully resisted the [siege of Brno](/wiki/Siege_of_Brno "Siege of Brno") in 1645, the only Moravian city that succeeded in defending itself from the Swedes, who were commanded by Field Marshal [Lennart Torstensson](/wiki/Lennart_Torstensson "Lennart Torstensson"). Brno subsequently served as the sole capital of the Margraviate of Moravia.

After the conclusion of the Thirty Years' War in 1648, Brno retained its status as the sole capital of the region. That was later confirmed by the Holy Roman Emperor [Joseph II](/wiki/Joseph_II,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Joseph II, Holy Roman Emperor") in 1782, and again in 1849 by the Moravian constitution.[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brno#endnote_Olomouc_also_had_the_legal_status_of_a_capital_city,_although_this_title_was_purely_honorary;_sometimes_it_was_referred_to_as_%22the_Secondary_Capital%22.) Today, the Moravian Land Tables are stored in the [Moravian Regional Archive](/w/index.php?title=Moravian_Regional_Archive&action=edit&redlink=1 "Moravian Regional Archive (page does not exist)") [[cs](https://cs.wikipedia.org/wiki/Moravsk%C3%BD_zemsk%C3%BD_archiv_v_Brn%C4%9B "cs:Moravský zemský archiv v Brně")] and are designated as a national cultural heritage site of the Czech Republic.[[57]](#cite_note-58) During the 17th century, [Špilberk Castle](/wiki/%C5%A0pilberk_Castle "Špilberk Castle") was rebuilt as a massive baroque [citadel](/wiki/Citadel "Citadel").[[58]](#cite_note-spilberkhist-59) Brno was besieged by the [Prussian Army](/wiki/Prussian_Army "Prussian Army") led by [Frederick the Great](/wiki/Frederick_the_Great "Frederick the Great") during the [War of the Austrian Succession](/wiki/War_of_the_Austrian_Succession "War of the Austrian Succession") in 1742, but the siege was ultimately unsuccessful. In 1777, the [bishopric of Brno](/wiki/Diocese_of_Brno "Diocese of Brno") was established by [Pope Pius VI](/wiki/Pope_Pius_VI "Pope Pius VI"), with [Mathias Franz Graf von Chorinsky Freiherr von Ledske](/wiki/Mathias_Franz_Graf_von_Chorinsky_Freiherr_von_Ledske "Mathias Franz Graf von Chorinsky Freiherr von Ledske") as its first bishop.[[38]](#cite_note-brnohist-38)[[41]](#cite_note-gemaart-41)[[b]](#cite_note-60)

During the 18th century, Brno experienced a significant [textile industry](/wiki/Textile_industry "Textile industry") boom, resulting in the rapid expansion of its suburbs and earning it the nickname "Moravian [Manchester](/wiki/Manchester "Manchester")".[[59]](#cite_note-61)[[60]](#cite_note-62) Brno flourished economically through the end of the 19th century. The city infrastructure, including [sewer](/wiki/Sewage_treatment "Sewage treatment") and [water supply](/wiki/Water_supply "Water supply") systems, was improved, and the fortifications around [Špilberk Castle](/wiki/%C5%A0pilberk_Castle "Špilberk Castle") were reinforced. Rapid [industrialisation](/wiki/Industrialisation "Industrialisation") brought about poor housing and working conditions for factory workers. [Child labour](/wiki/Child_labour "Child labour"), [tuberculosis](/wiki/Tuberculosis "Tuberculosis"), inhumanely long shifts, and unequal relations between employees and employers were commonplace.[[61]](#cite_note-63)

### 19th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brno&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 19th century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ab/La_bataille_d%27Austerlitz._2_decembre_1805_%28Fran%C3%A7ois_G%C3%A9rard%29_%28cropped%2C_1to1_portrait%29.jpg/250px-La_bataille_d%27Austerlitz._2_decembre_1805_%28Fran%C3%A7ois_G%C3%A9rard%29_%28cropped%2C_1to1_portrait%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:La_bataille_d%27Austerlitz._2_decembre_1805_(Fran%C3%A7ois_G%C3%A9rard)_(cropped,_1to1_portrait).jpg)

Battle of Austerlitz, 2 December 1805

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/50/Lo_Spielberg_%28xilografia%29.jpg/250px-Lo_Spielberg_%28xilografia%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lo_Spielberg_(xilografia).jpg)

Špilberk Castle c. 1897

In December 1805, the [Battle of Austerlitz](/wiki/Battle_of_Austerlitz "Battle of Austerlitz") was fought near the city; the battle is also known as the "Battle of the Three Emperors". Although Brno itself was not directly involved in the battle, the city was under French occupation, which conferred significant hardship on the local population. In the wake of the hostilities, there was a devastating [typhoid fever](/wiki/Typhoid_fever "Typhoid fever") outbreak due to the mild winter temperatures that year.[[62]](#cite_note-64) The French Emperor [Napoleon Bonaparte](/wiki/Napoleon_Bonaparte "Napoleon Bonaparte") stayed in Brno from 20 November to 12 December 1805.[[63]](#cite_note-65)[[64]](#cite_note-66) The French returned in 1809 and occupied the city for four months after the [Battle of Znaim](/wiki/Battle_of_Znaim "Battle of Znaim").[[65]](#cite_note-67)[[66]](#cite_note-68)[[67]](#cite_note-69) Napoleon ordered the fortification around Špilberk Castle dismantled and leveled, and the Castle lost its military significance as a fortress.[[68]](#cite_note-70) These events are the theme of re-enactments that attract an international audience every year.[[69]](#cite_note-71)

In 1839, the first train arrived in Brno from Vienna via the [Emperor Ferdinand Northern Railway](/wiki/Emperor_Ferdinand_Northern_Railway "Emperor Ferdinand Northern Railway"). That marked the beginning of [rail transport in the Czech Republic](/wiki/Rail_transport_in_the_Czech_Republic "Rail transport in the Czech Republic") and [Austria](/wiki/Rail_transport_in_Austria "Rail transport in Austria").[[70]](#cite_note-thefirsttrain-72) Between 1859 and 1864, city fortifications were almost completely removed. In 1869, a [horsecar](/wiki/Horsecar "Horsecar") service began operating in Brno, marking it the first [tram service](/wiki/Tram "Tram") in what would later become the Czech Republic.[[71]](#cite_note-dpmbhistory-73) [Gregor Mendel](/wiki/Gregor_Mendel "Gregor Mendel"), an Augustinian monk and geneticist, conducted groundbreaking [genetics](/wiki/Genetics "Genetics") experiments with pea plants at [St. Thomas's Abbey](/wiki/St._Thomas%27s_Abbey,_Brno "St. Thomas's Abbey, Brno") in Brno during the 1850s.[[72]](#cite_note-74) The monastery served as a local hub for education, culture, and the arts, with a well-stocked library and other amenities. Mendel's work was not recognized during his lifetime, and he abandoned it after he was elected abbot of the monastery in 1868. Brno is home to [Mendel University](/wiki/Mendel_University_in_Brno "Mendel University in Brno"), which has been located there since the mid-1990s, when the former University of Agriculture, the nation's oldest agricultural school, was renamed in his honour.

[Tomáš Masaryk](/wiki/Tom%C3%A1%C5%A1_Masaryk "Tomáš Masaryk"), the first president and founding father of Czechoslovakia, attended a German-language grammar school (German: *Deutsches Ober-Gymnasium*) in Brno between 1865 and 1869.[[73]](#cite_note-75) His school served as a military hospital during the [Austro-Prussian War](/wiki/Austro-Prussian_War "Austro-Prussian War") of 1866, which led to the creation of the dual monarchy of [Austria-Hungary](/wiki/Austria-Hungary "Austria-Hungary"). Although no major conflict took place in Brno during the war, the Prussian Army occupied the city following the [Battle of Königgrätz](/wiki/Battle_of_K%C3%B6niggr%C3%A4tz "Battle of Königgrätz"). Several days later, a cholera epidemic that claimed the lives of more than 1,400 residents broke out.[[74]](#cite_note-76)[[75]](#cite_note-77) Masaryk later became a proponent of a second Czech university, alongside [Charles University](/wiki/Charles_University "Charles University"), which was established in Brno in 1919 and named after him.[[76]](#cite_note-78) The [Masaryk Circuit](/wiki/Masaryk_Circuit "Masaryk Circuit") and Masarykova Street, connecting Liberty Square and the train station, are also named in his honour.

[Leoš Janáček](/wiki/Leo%C5%A1_Jan%C3%A1%C4%8Dek "Leoš Janáček"), ranked among most-performed opera composers in the world, accepted a chorister scholarship at [St. Thomas's Abbey](/wiki/St._Thomas%27s_Abbey,_Brno "St. Thomas's Abbey, Brno") in 1865.[[77]](#cite_note-79) He would spend the rest of his life in Brno, except for the period between 1872 and 1881 when he pursued advanced musical studies in Prague, Vienna, and Leipzig. He founded the [Brno Organ School](/wiki/Brno_Conservatory "Brno Conservatory"), which is now part of the [Janáček Academy of Music and Performing Arts](/wiki/Jan%C3%A1%C4%8Dek_Academy_of_Performing_Arts "Janáček Academy of Performing Arts") (JAMU), upon his return in 1881. Janáček had a profound impact on Brno and its cultural life. His legacy helped transform the city into a major cultural hub.[[78]](#cite_note-80)[[79]](#cite_note-81) Owing to his significance to Brno's history, several places and institutions have been named in his honour. In addition to JAMU, there is also the [Janáček Theatre](/wiki/Jan%C3%A1%C4%8Dek_Theatre "Janáček Theatre"), an [opera house](/wiki/Opera_house "Opera house") and the largest of the three theatres belonging to the [National Theatre Brno](/wiki/National_Theatre_Brno "National Theatre Brno").[[80]](#cite_note-82)[[81]](#cite_note-83)

### 20th century and Greater Brno

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brno&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 20th century and Greater Brno")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5d/Brno%2C_hlavn%C3%AD_n%C3%A1dra%C5%BE%C3%AD_a_tramvaje.jpg/250px-Brno%2C_hlavn%C3%AD_n%C3%A1dra%C5%BE%C3%AD_a_tramvaje.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brno,_hlavn%C3%AD_n%C3%A1dra%C5%BE%C3%AD_a_tramvaje.jpg)

Main railway station in 1901

At the turn of the 20th century, roughly two-thirds of Brno's residents [spoke German](/wiki/Moravian_Germans "Moravian Germans"), and its suburbs and neighbouring towns were predominantly Czech-speaking.[[82]](#cite_note-84)[[83]](#cite_note-brunna-85) Everyday life in the city was bilingual. The local German dialect, known as *Brünnerisch*, incorporated elements from both languages.[[83]](#cite_note-brunna-85) The end of [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") brought about the partition of [Austria-Hungary](/wiki/Austria-Hungary "Austria-Hungary"), and more than three million Germans found themselves separated from Austria and under Czech control. After the [Czechoslovak Republic](/wiki/First_Czechoslovak_Republic "First Czechoslovak Republic") was proclaimed on 28 October, 1918, the [Sudeten Germans](/wiki/Sudeten_Germans "Sudeten Germans") asserted their right to [self-determination](/wiki/Self-determination "Self-determination") and peacefully opposed the Czech military taking control of their homeland. Fighting broke out sporadically, resulting in casualties.[[84]](#cite_note-:4-86) [Moravia](/wiki/Moravia "Moravia") contained patches of "landlocked" German territory to the north and south. German-[language islands](/wiki/Language_island "Language island") and towns inhabited by German-speaking populations, surrounded by Czech speakers, were common, much like in Brno.[[44]](#cite_note-:5-44)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9b/Czechoslovakia_1930_linguistic_map_-_en.svg/250px-Czechoslovakia_1930_linguistic_map_-_en.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Czechoslovakia_1930_linguistic_map_-_en.svg)

Linguistic map of Czechoslovakia in 1930

Until 1918, the city of Brno was significantly smaller, and in administrative terms, it consisted only of the city centre. In 1919, two neighboring towns, [Královo Pole](/wiki/Kr%C3%A1lovo_Pole "Královo Pole") and [Husovice](/wiki/Husovice "Husovice"), along with 21 other surrounding municipalities, were annexed by Brno, creating Greater Brno (Czech: *Velké Brno*) in an effort to dilute the German-speaking population majority, which comprised approximately 55,000 residents, by adding Czech speakers from the city's vicinity.[[85]](#cite_note-Hahn-87) A significant portion of the German-speaking population was Jewish, comprising nearly 12,000 individuals, including several of the city's better-known personalities, who made a substantial contribution to the city's cultural life.[[85]](#cite_note-Hahn-87) Greater Brno was almost seven times larger in area, with a population of about 222,000 – before that, Brno proper had about 130,000 inhabitants.[[86]](#cite_note-Greater_Brno-88)[[87]](#cite_note-89)[[88]](#cite_note-90)[[89]](#cite_note-91)

The size of Brno's German population was virtually unchanged in the [1930 census](/wiki/Demographics_of_Czechoslovakia#Religious_affiliations_in_1930 "Demographics of Czechoslovakia"), the last census before the war, when 200,000 residents declared Czech nationality, and approximately 52,000 identified as German. Both groups included Jewish citizens.[[85]](#cite_note-Hahn-87) Although Czechoslovakia experienced a peaceful and prosperous decade during the 1920s, ethnic tensions escalated in the 1930s following the onset of the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression"). Hitler's rise to power further inflamed the situation. Particularly when the [Sudeten German Party](/wiki/Sudeten_German_Party "Sudeten German Party") became the second-largest party in Czechoslovakia, and it became known that the party received financial support from Berlin.[[84]](#cite_note-:4-86)[[90]](#cite_note-92) During the [interwar period](/wiki/Interwar_period "Interwar period"), Brno served as the capital of the administrative region of Moravia (Czech: *Země Moravská*) from 1921 to 1928, and as the capital of Moravia-Silesia (Czech: *Země Moravskoslezská*) from 1928 to 1948. The city emerged as the preeminent site of modernist architecture during this period.[[91]](#cite_note-93)[[92]](#cite_note-94)[[93]](#cite_note-95)

### World War II

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brno&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: World War II")]

Main article: [Protectorate of Bohemia and Moravia](/wiki/Protectorate_of_Bohemia_and_Moravia "Protectorate of Bohemia and Moravia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c6/Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-2004-0813-500%2C_Deutsche_Truppen_in_Br%C3%BCnn.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-2004-0813-500%2C_Deutsche_Truppen_in_Br%C3%BCnn.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-2004-0813-500,_Deutsche_Truppen_in_Br%C3%BCnn.jpg)

Part of the civilian population welcomes German troops with the [Nazi salute](/wiki/Nazi_salute "Nazi salute") in Brno, 16 March 1939.

During the [German occupation of the Czech lands](/wiki/Occupation_of_Czechoslovakia_(1938%E2%80%931945) "Occupation of Czechoslovakia (1938–1945)") between 1939 and 1945, all Czech universities were closed by the Nazis, including those in Brno. The [Faculty of Law](/wiki/Masaryk_University "Masaryk University") became the local headquarters of the [Gestapo](/wiki/Gestapo "Gestapo"), and the Nazis used the Kounic Residence Hall at Masaryk University as a political prison for anti-fascists. Along with the [firing range in Kobylisy](/wiki/Kobylisy_Shooting_Range "Kobylisy Shooting Range"), the Kounic dormitories were the most frequently used execution grounds in the [Protectorate](/wiki/Protectorate_of_Bohemia_and_Moravia "Protectorate of Bohemia and Moravia").[[30]](#cite_note-Burian-30) About 35,000 Czechs and some American and British [POWs](/wiki/Prisoner_of_war "Prisoner of war") were imprisoned and tortured there; about 800 civilians were executed or died.[[94]](#cite_note-96)[[95]](#cite_note-97)[[96]](#cite_note-98)[[97]](#cite_note-99) The executions were public.[[98]](#cite_note-100)[[99]](#cite_note-101)[[100]](#cite_note-102) The Nazis also operated a [subcamp](/wiki/List_of_subcamps_of_Auschwitz "List of subcamps of Auschwitz") of the [Auschwitz concentration camp](/wiki/Auschwitz_concentration_camp "Auschwitz concentration camp"), which mainly held Polish prisoners, an internment camp for [Romani people](/wiki/Romani_people "Romani people") in the city, and a [forced labour](/wiki/Forced_labour_under_German_rule_during_World_War_II "Forced labour under German rule during World War II") "re-education" camp in the present-day district of [Dvorska](/wiki/Dvorska_(Brno) "Dvorska (Brno)").[[101]](#cite_note-103)[[102]](#cite_note-104)[[103]](#cite_note-105)

Between 1941 and 1942, [transports](/wiki/Holocaust_train "Holocaust train") from Brno deported 10,081 Jews to the [Theresienstadt (Terezín)](/wiki/Theresienstadt_Ghetto "Theresienstadt Ghetto") concentration camp.[[104]](#cite_note-106) At least another 960 people, mostly of [mixed ancestry](/wiki/Anti-miscegenation_laws#Nazi_Germany "Anti-miscegenation laws"), followed in 1943 and 1944. From Terezín, many of them were sent to the [Auschwitz concentration camp](/wiki/Auschwitz_concentration_camp "Auschwitz concentration camp"), the [Minsk Ghetto](/wiki/Minsk_Ghetto "Minsk Ghetto"), [Rejowiec](/wiki/Rejowiec,_Lublin_Voivodeship "Rejowiec, Lublin Voivodeship"), and other [ghettos](/wiki/Ghettos_in_Nazi-occupied_Europe "Ghettos in Nazi-occupied Europe") and [concentration camps](/wiki/Nazi_concentration_camps "Nazi concentration camps"). Although Terezín was not an [extermination camp](/wiki/Extermination_camp "Extermination camp"), 995 deportees from Brno perished there. Only 1,033 people from over 11,000 returned after the war.[[105]](#cite_note-107) In 1944 and 1945, the city centre and several industrial facilities, such as the [Československá zbrojovka](/wiki/Zbrojovka_Brno "Zbrojovka Brno") small arms factory and the [Flugmotorenwerke Zweigwerk](/wiki/Flugmotorenwerke_Ostmark "Flugmotorenwerke Ostmark") aircraft engine factory, were targeted by [Allied bombing raids](/wiki/Strategic_bombing_during_World_War_II "Strategic bombing during World War II").

Air strikes and artillery fire killed some 1,200 people and destroyed 1,278 buildings.[[106]](#cite_note-Zweigwerk-108)[[107]](#cite_note-109) More than 12,000 buildings, or about half of the city's building stock, were damaged.[[108]](#cite_note-110)[[109]](#cite_note-111) In the wake of the occupation by the [Red Army](/wiki/Red_Army "Red Army") on 26 April 1945 and the end of the war, President [Edvard Beneš](/wiki/Edvard_Bene%C5%A1 "Edvard Beneš") arrived in Brno and delivered a speech demanding the [expulsion of Germans](/wiki/Expulsion_of_Germans_from_Czechoslovakia "Expulsion of Germans from Czechoslovakia").[[110]](#cite_note-112)[[111]](#cite_note-113)[[112]](#cite_note-114) Subsequently, beginning on 31 May 1945, remaining German inhabitants, comprising over 20,000 individuals, were [marched](/wiki/Brno_death_march "Brno death march") 64 kilometres (40 miles) to the [Austrian](/wiki/Allied-occupied_Austria "Allied-occupied Austria") border.[[113]](#cite_note-115)[[114]](#cite_note-116)[[115]](#cite_note-117) According to testimony collected by German sources, about 5,200 of them died during the march.[[116]](#cite_note-118) Later Czech estimates put the death toll at about 1,700, with most of the fatalities attributed to an epidemic of [shigellosis](/wiki/Shigellosis "Shigellosis").[[117]](#cite_note-119) In 2015, seventy years after the end of World War II, the city council issued a formal apology and expressed regret about the expulsion of German residents.[[118]](#cite_note-120) The event is commemorated every year.[[119]](#cite_note-121)

### Communist Era and post-1989 transformation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brno&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Communist Era and post-1989 transformation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7c/Brno_listopad_3.jpg/330px-Brno_listopad_3.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brno_listopad_3.jpg)

Demonstration at Náměstí Svobody, 20 November 1989

Shortly after the [communist coup d'état](/wiki/1948_Czechoslovak_coup_d%27%C3%A9tat "1948 Czechoslovak coup d'état") in 1948, the new regime abolished the traditional system of regional governance in an effort to centralise power in Prague, effectively ending Brno's role as the capital of Moravia.[[120]](#cite_note-122) Moravia was divided into administrative districts, with Brno becoming the administrative centre of what is now the [South Moravian Region](/wiki/South_Moravian_Region "South Moravian Region").[[121]](#cite_note-zakon2081948-123) Throughout the Communist Era, Brno's economy relied on heavy manufacturing and machine-building.[[122]](#cite_note-124)[[123]](#cite_note-125) Large prefab concrete housing estates (*[paneláks](/wiki/Panel%C3%A1k "Panelák")*) were constructed during the 1960s and 1970s in [Bohunice](/wiki/Brno-Bohunice "Brno-Bohunice"), [Líšeň](/wiki/Brno-L%C3%AD%C5%A1e%C5%88 "Brno-Líšeň"), [Bystrc](/wiki/Brno-Bystrc "Brno-Bystrc"), and [Vinohrady](/wiki/Vinohrady "Vinohrady"). Many historic buildings fell into disrepair or were demolished as part of urban redevelopment.[[124]](#cite_note-126)

Following the [Velvet Revolution](/wiki/Velvet_Revolution "Velvet Revolution") of 1989, Brno's economy underwent a substantial transformation. Its workforce transitioned from heavy manufacturing to services and light industry, and Brno emerged as an information technology and research hub of the Czech Republic.[[125]](#cite_note-127) New industrial parks, such as CTPark Brno in [Černovická terasa](/w/index.php?title=%C4%8Cernovick%C3%A1_terasa&action=edit&redlink=1 "Černovická terasa (page does not exist)") [[cs](https://cs.wikipedia.org/wiki/%C4%8Cernovick%C3%A1_terasa "cs:Černovická terasa")], were developed on the city's outskirts.[[126]](#cite_note-128) Today, the wider Brno region is a global centre for electron microscopy and cybersecurity development.[[127]](#cite_note-129)[[128]](#cite_note-130) The city has also experienced a considerable cultural renaissance and historical renewal.[[129]](#cite_note-131)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brno&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Geography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/Autumn_Marian_Valley.jpg/250px-Autumn_Marian_Valley.jpg)](/wiki/File:Autumn_Marian_Valley.jpg)

The Marian Valley in Líšeň

Brno is situated at the confluence of the [Svitava](/wiki/Svitava_(river) "Svitava (river)") and [Svratka](/wiki/Svratka_(river) "Svratka (river)") rivers, located between the [Bohemian-Moravian Highlands](/wiki/Bohemian-Moravian_Highlands "Bohemian-Moravian Highlands") and the [Dyje–Svratka Valley](/wiki/Dyje%E2%80%93Svratka_Valley "Dyje–Svratka Valley") in the southeastern part of the Czech Republic. The city is at the crossroads of ancient trade routes, which have linked northern and southern Europe since time immemorial. Brno is historically closely connected with Vienna, which lies 110 km (68 mi) to the south.[[130]](#cite_note-guocz-132) The city is 21.5 km (13.4 mi) in diameter east to west with a total area of 230 km2 (89 sq mi).[[130]](#cite_note-guocz-132) Wooded hills surround Brno on three sides. About 28% of the city area, or 6,379 ha (15,763 acres), is forested.

The city is relatively low-lying, with elevations ranging between 190 m (623 ft) and 497 m (1,631 ft). The highest peak within the municipal area is Kopeček Hill, at 479 m (1,572 ft), and the highest elevation overall is [Útěchov](/wiki/Brno-%C3%9At%C4%9Bchov "Brno-Útěchov"), located on the border with [Vranov](/wiki/Vranov_(Brno-Country_District) "Vranov (Brno-Country District)"). Brno is located within the [Danube basin](/wiki/Danube_basin "Danube basin"). The length of the Svratka River waterway through the city is about 29 km (18 mi), and the Svitava River's waterway is about 13 km (8 mi).[[2]](#cite_note-witwib-2) Several smaller streams flow through Brno, including the [Veverka Creek](/w/index.php?title=Veverka_Creek&action=edit&redlink=1 "Veverka Creek (page does not exist)") [[cs](https://cs.wikipedia.org/wiki/Veverka_(potok) "cs:Veverka (potok)")], the [Ponávka](/w/index.php?title=Pon%C3%A1vka&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ponávka (page does not exist)") [[cs](https://cs.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pon%C3%A1vka "cs:Ponávka")], and the [Říčka](/w/index.php?title=%C5%98%C3%AD%C4%8Dka&action=edit&redlink=1 "Říčka (page does not exist)") [[cs](https://cs.wikipedia.org/wiki/%C5%98%C3%AD%C4%8Dka_(p%C5%99%C3%ADtok_Litavy) "cs:Říčka (přítok Litavy)")]. Of note is the [Brno Reservoir](/wiki/Brno_Reservoir "Brno Reservoir"), as well as numerous ponds, and other standing bodies of water, such as the [reservoirs](/wiki/Reservoir "Reservoir") in the [Marian Valley](/w/index.php?title=Marian_Valley&action=edit&redlink=1 "Marian Valley (page does not exist)") [[cs](https://cs.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mari%C3%A1nsk%C3%A9_%C3%BAdol%C3%AD_(Brno) "cs:Mariánské údolí (Brno)")][[131]](#cite_note-133) and the fishpond [Žebětínský rybník](/w/index.php?title=%C5%BDeb%C4%9Bt%C3%ADnsk%C3%BD_rybn%C3%ADk&action=edit&redlink=1 "Žebětínský rybník (page does not exist)") [[cs](https://cs.wikipedia.org/wiki/%C5%BDeb%C4%9Bt%C3%ADnsk%C3%BD_rybn%C3%ADk "cs:Žebětínský rybník")].

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brno&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Climate")]

Brno has a moderate climate and moderate air quality.[[2]](#cite_note-witwib-2)[[132]](#cite_note-134)[[133]](#cite_note-135) No severe weather events or disasters have been recorded.[[2]](#cite_note-witwib-2) Under the [Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification"), Brno has an [oceanic climate](/wiki/Oceanic_climate "Oceanic climate") (*Cfb*) for −3 °C original isoterm,[[134]](#cite_note-136) but near the −2.5 °C average temperature in January, the coldest month. It is classified as a [humid continental climate](/wiki/Humid_continental_climate "Humid continental climate") (*Dfb*) in the updated classification, characterized by cold winters and warm to hot summers.[[135]](#cite_note-Peel-137) A warming trend has been observed over the past two decades, and summer days with temperatures above 30 °C (86 °F) are relatively common.[[136]](#cite_note-138) The average temperature is 9.4 °C (49 °F), and the average annual precipitation is approximately 505 mm (19.88 in) with an average of 150 rainy days. Average annual sunshine hours are 1,771 hours, and the prevailing wind direction is northwest.[[2]](#cite_note-witwib-2) Weather data from 1961 to 1990 is tabulated in the weather box below.

## Administration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brno&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Administration")]

Main articles: [Administrative divisions of Brno](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_Brno "Administrative divisions of Brno") and [List of Mayors of Brno](/wiki/List_of_Mayors_of_Brno "List of Mayors of Brno")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2d/Justi%C4%8Dn%C3%AD_pal%C3%A1c_v_Brn%C4%9B.jpg/250px-Justi%C4%8Dn%C3%AD_pal%C3%A1c_v_Brn%C4%9B.jpg)](/wiki/File:Justi%C4%8Dn%C3%AD_pal%C3%A1c_v_Brn%C4%9B.jpg)

The Palace of Justice, seat of the regional court

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/11/M%C4%9Bstsk%C3%A9_%C4%8D%C3%A1sti_Brna_a_jejich_znaky.png/250px-M%C4%9Bstsk%C3%A9_%C4%8D%C3%A1sti_Brna_a_jejich_znaky.png)](/wiki/File:M%C4%9Bstsk%C3%A9_%C4%8D%C3%A1sti_Brna_a_jejich_znaky.png)

[Administrative divisions of Brno](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_Brno "Administrative divisions of Brno") and their coats of arms

Brno is a [statutory city](/wiki/Statutory_city_(Czech_Republic) "Statutory city (Czech Republic)"), consisting of 29 administrative divisions known as city districts.[[148]](#cite_note-150) The highest body of self-government is the Brno City Assembly.[[149]](#cite_note-TheAssemblyOfTheCity-151) The city is headed up by the [mayor](/wiki/Mayor "Mayor"), who has the right to use the mayor's insignia and represents the city externally. As of 2025, the mayor is [Markéta Vaňková](/w/index.php?title=Mark%C3%A9ta_Va%C5%88kov%C3%A1&action=edit&redlink=1 "Markéta Vaňková (page does not exist)") [[cs](https://cs.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mark%C3%A9ta_Va%C5%88kov%C3%A1 "cs:Markéta Vaňková")] of the [Civic Democratic Party](/wiki/Civic_Democratic_Party_(Czech_Republic) "Civic Democratic Party (Czech Republic)") (ODS).[[150]](#cite_note-152) The executive body is the city council and local councils of the city districts; the city council has 11 members, including the mayor and four deputies.[[151]](#cite_note-153) The assembly of the city elects the mayor and other members of the city council, establishes the local police, and is also entitled to grant citizenship of honour and the Awards of the City of Brno.[[149]](#cite_note-TheAssemblyOfTheCity-151)

The head of the Assembly of the City of Brno in personal matters is the Chief Executive, who, according to certain special regulations, carries out the function of employer of the other members of the city management.[[152]](#cite_note-154) The Chief Executive is directly responsible to the mayor.[[153]](#cite_note-155)

Brno proper forms a separate district, the [Brno-City District](/wiki/Brno-City_District "Brno-City District"), surrounded by the [Brno-Country District](/wiki/Brno-Country_District "Brno-Country District"). The city is divided into 29 administrative divisions (city districts) and consists of 48 [cadastral areas](/wiki/Cadastre "Cadastre"). The "Brno-City District" and "Brno-Country District" are not to be confused with the "city districts" of Brno. The city districts of Brno vary widely by both population and area. The most populous city district of Brno is [Brno-Centre](/wiki/Brno-Centre "Brno-Centre"), which has over 91,000 residents, and the least populous is [Brno-Ořešín](/wiki/Brno-O%C5%99e%C5%A1%C3%ADn "Brno-Ořešín") and [Brno-Útěchov](/wiki/Brno-%C3%9At%C4%9Bchov "Brno-Útěchov"), with about 500 residents. By area, the largest district is [Brno-Bystrc](/wiki/Brno-Bystrc "Brno-Bystrc") (27.24 square kilometres; 10.52 sq mi) and the smallest one is [Brno-Nový Lískovec](/wiki/Brno-Nov%C3%BD_L%C3%ADskovec "Brno-Nový Lískovec") (1.66 square kilometres; 0.64 sq mi). Brno is the home to the highest courts of the [Czech judiciary](/wiki/Judiciary_of_the_Czech_Republic "Judiciary of the Czech Republic"). The [Supreme Court](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_Czech_Republic "Supreme Court of the Czech Republic") is on Burešova Street,[[154]](#cite_note-sccz-156) the [Supreme Administrative Court](/wiki/Supreme_Administrative_Court_of_the_Czech_Republic "Supreme Administrative Court of the Czech Republic") is on Moravské náměstí (English: Moravian Square),[[155]](#cite_note-saccz-157) the [Constitutional Court](/wiki/Constitutional_Court_of_the_Czech_Republic "Constitutional Court of the Czech Republic") is on Joštova Street,[[156]](#cite_note-158) and the Supreme Public Prosecutor's Office of the Czech Republic is on Jezuitská Street.[[157]](#cite_note-ppocz-159)

## Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brno&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Demographics")]

According to the 2021 census, Brno had a population of 398,510 inhabitants.[[158]](#cite_note-2021-census-population-160) The city experienced the most significant population growth in the 19th century during the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution") and after [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), when it annexed nearly two dozen surrounding municipalities to create Greater Brno.[[86]](#cite_note-Greater_Brno-88)

### Ethnicity and nationality

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brno&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Ethnicity and nationality")]

The largest ethnic groups in the 2021 census were [Czechs](/wiki/Czechs "Czechs") (44.60%), [Moravians](/wiki/Moravians "Moravians") (14.08%), [Slovaks](/wiki/Slovaks "Slovaks") (1.74%), [Ukrainians](/wiki/Ukrainians "Ukrainians") (1.04%), Russians (0.36%), [Vietnamese](/wiki/Vietnamese_people "Vietnamese people") (0.35%), and [Poles](/wiki/Polish_people "Polish people") (0.10%). About 26% of respondents did not specify their nationality. In the 2001 census, with different reporting requirements, 76.1% identified as Czechs and 18.7% as Moravians.[[160]](#cite_note-1980-2021-census-data-162) And in the 1991 census, 35.8% identified as Czechs and 60.9% as Moravians.[[161]](#cite_note-1980-2021-census-demographics-163) The outcome of the 1980 census is included for historical completeness. Censuses conducted under communist rule in Czechoslovakia were heavily politicized, particularly concerning the registration of nationality and religion. Beyond manipulating the categories and responses, the communist regime also controlled the dissemination of the census data itself.[[162]](#cite_note-164)
