## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brno&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Economy")]

After World War II, the site of [Flugmotorenwerke Zweigwerk](/wiki/Flugmotorenwerke_Ostmark "Flugmotorenwerke Ostmark") in [Brno-Líšeň](/wiki/Brno-L%C3%AD%C5%A1e%C5%88 "Brno-Líšeň") became the [Zetor](/wiki/Zetor "Zetor") factory.[[106]](#cite_note-Zweigwerk-108) Though it initially operated as a subsidiary of [Zbrojovka](/wiki/Zbrojovka_Brno "Zbrojovka Brno"), it became an independent manufacturer of tractors and agricultural equipment.[[164]](#cite_note-166) At its peak in the 1970s, Zetor manufactured more than 70,000 tractors annually and employed thousands of people.[[165]](#cite_note-167) The iron foundry, which was part of the site, was later acquired by the German company [Heunisch-Guss](/w/index.php?title=Heunisch-Guss&action=edit&redlink=1 "Heunisch-Guss (page does not exist)") [[de](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Heunisch-Guss "de:Heunisch-Guss")]. Zbrojovka itself, which once employed over 10,000 people, went bankrupt in the early 2000s and was subsequently acquired by [Česká Zbrojovka](/wiki/Colt_CZ_Group "Colt CZ Group").[[166]](#cite_note-168)[[167]](#cite_note-169)[[168]](#cite_note-170)[[169]](#cite_note-171) The entire group is now named [Colt CZ Group SE](/wiki/Colt_CZ_Group "Colt CZ Group") after it acquired [Colt Holding](/wiki/Colt%27s_Manufacturing_Company "Colt's Manufacturing Company") in 2021 and rebranded itself.[[170]](#cite_note-172)[[171]](#cite_note-173)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8b/Building_of_JIC.jpg/330px-Building_of_JIC.jpg)](/wiki/File:Building_of_JIC.jpg)

[South Moravian Innovation Centre](/wiki/South_Moravian_Innovation_Centre "South Moravian Innovation Centre")

There has been a significant shift in the local economy since the [Velvet Revolution](/wiki/Velvet_Revolution "Velvet Revolution") in 1989. Many new companies were created or spun off from state enterprises as part of the [privatization process](/wiki/Voucher_privatization "Voucher privatization"), while several others ceased to exist entirely.[[172]](#cite_note-174) Before 1990, manufacturing and machine-building companies were pillars of Brno's economy. Since then, the city's economy has largely realigned itself with market demand for light industry, logistics, and services. Brno later gained some traction in newer engineering industries, especially in [software development](/wiki/Software_development "Software development"). After the turn of the millennium, foreign technology companies began to establish subsidiaries in Brno. Several Czech companies, with both local and global reach, also have headquarters in the city.[[173]](#cite_note-175)[[174]](#cite_note-176)

Notable companies based in Brno include [Gen Digital](/wiki/Gen_Digital "Gen Digital") (one of the headquarters, brand [AVG Technologies](/wiki/AVG_Technologies "AVG Technologies") still used),[[175]](#cite_note-177) [Kyndryl](/wiki/Kyndryl "Kyndryl") (Collaborative),[[176]](#cite_note-178)[[177]](#cite_note-179) [AT&T](/wiki/AT%26T "AT&T"), [Honeywell](/wiki/Honeywell "Honeywell") (Global Design Center),[[178]](#cite_note-180) [Siemens](/wiki/Siemens "Siemens"),[[179]](#cite_note-181) [Red Hat](/wiki/Red_Hat "Red Hat") (Czech headquarters),[[180]](#cite_note-182) an office of [Zebra Technologies](/wiki/Zebra_Technologies "Zebra Technologies"),[[181]](#cite_note-183) and formerly [Silicon Graphics International](/wiki/Silicon_Graphics_International "Silicon Graphics International") (Czech headquarters).[[182]](#cite_note-184) In recent years, knowledge-based drivers of economic growth, including science, research, and education, have become increasingly critical to the city's economy. Examples include AdMaS (Advanced Materials, Structures, and Technologies)[[183]](#cite_note-185) or CETOCOEN (Center for Research on Toxic Substances in the Environment).[[184]](#cite_note-186) The city cultivates this sector via its [South Moravian Innovation Centre](/wiki/South_Moravian_Innovation_Centre "South Moravian Innovation Centre") and the [VUT](/wiki/Brno_University_of_Technology "Brno University of Technology") Technology Incubator.[[185]](#cite_note-187)

## Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brno&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Transport")]

See also: [Trams in Brno](/wiki/Trams_in_Brno "Trams in Brno")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/%C5%A0alina_%C4%8CD_ComfortJet%2C_Husitsk%C3%A1.jpg/250px-%C5%A0alina_%C4%8CD_ComfortJet%2C_Husitsk%C3%A1.jpg)](/wiki/File:%C5%A0alina_%C4%8CD_ComfortJet,_Husitsk%C3%A1.jpg)

Tram service is the backbone of public transport in Brno.

Brno's public transit network is extensive. There are 11 [tram](/wiki/Trams_in_Brno "Trams in Brno") lines, 13 [trolleybus](/wiki/Trolleybus "Trolleybus") lines, and 47 bus routes.[[186]](#cite_note-:1-188)[[187]](#cite_note-189) Trams, locally known as *šaliny*, are so ubiquitous that Brno has been nicknamed Šalingrad.[[188]](#cite_note-190)[[189]](#cite_note-191) They first appeared in 1869 with the premiere of a [horse-drawn tram](/wiki/Horse-drawn_tram "Horse-drawn tram") on the territory of the Czech Republic. The trolleybus fleet, with 157 vehicles, is the largest in the country.[[190]](#cite_note-192)[[191]](#cite_note-193) Local public transit integrates with regional transit into one system, IDS JMK,[[186]](#cite_note-:1-188) and links surrounding municipalities to the city.[[192]](#cite_note-:2-194) Its operator, [Dopravní podnik města Brna](/w/index.php?title=Dopravn%C3%AD_podnik_m%C4%9Bsta_Brna&action=edit&redlink=1 "Dopravní podnik města Brna (page does not exist)") [[cs](https://cs.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dopravn%C3%AD_podnik_m%C4%9Bsta_Brna "cs:Dopravní podnik města Brna")], also operates a ferry on the [Brno Reservoir](/wiki/Brno_Reservoir "Brno Reservoir").[[193]](#cite_note-195) A visitor [minibus](/wiki/Minibus "Minibus") provides sightseeing tours of the city.[[194]](#cite_note-196) In 2024, more than half of Brno's residents relied exclusively or almost exclusively on public transit. Approximately one-third of the population relied on personal motor vehicles. Pedestrians and cyclists/scooter riders accounted for 10% combined.[[195]](#cite_note-:3-197) The base 60-minute fare, valid for rides within the city, is 25 CZK (approx. €1).[[196]](#cite_note-198)

Tickets can be purchased at vending machines, at newsstands, through the operator's website or app, or through SMS from a domestic phone number. Various discounts, subsidies, and exemptions, including discounted monthly, quarterly, or annual passes (Czech: *Šalinkarta*), are available.[[192]](#cite_note-:2-194) In 2022, about 32,000 residents applied for a subsidy available on the annual pass. That discounted the pass from CZK 4,750 (€195) to CZK 3,325 (€137). The city allocated CZK 54 million (€2.2 million) for this benefit. With the annual pass, another adult and up to three children under 15 ride free on weekends.[[197]](#cite_note-199) In 2022, Dopravní podnik města Brna converted to electronic-only passes. In 2023, approximately 153,000 prepaid passes were sold, and 70% of passengers used a Šalinkarta pass.[[198]](#cite_note-200)[[199]](#cite_note-201) Brno operates a night-time bus service, which is unique in the Czech Republic for its regular central meeting point of all lines. In its first 25 years of operation it was used by more than 150 million passengers. The busiest nights are Friday and Saturday, when 22 buses serving 11 bus routes transport approximately 20,000 passengers.[[200]](#cite_note-202)[[201]](#cite_note-203)[[202]](#cite_note-204)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/Brno_hlavn%C3%AD_n%C3%A1dra%C5%BE%C3%AD_1.jpg/330px-Brno_hlavn%C3%AD_n%C3%A1dra%C5%BE%C3%AD_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brno_hlavn%C3%AD_n%C3%A1dra%C5%BE%C3%AD_1.jpg)

[Brno hlavní nádraží](/wiki/Brno_hlavn%C3%AD_n%C3%A1dra%C5%BE%C3%AD "Brno hlavní nádraží") railway station

Railway service began in 1839 on the [Brno–Vienna line](/wiki/North_railway_line "North railway line"), the first operational train line in the Czech lands and the former Austro-Hungarian Empire.[[70]](#cite_note-thefirsttrain-72) Today, Brno is an international railway hub with two train stations, several intermediate stops, and 24 regional train lines.[[203]](#cite_note-205)[[186]](#cite_note-:1-188) There are 13 direct rail connections to national destinations and 21 non-stop international routes.[[204]](#cite_note-206) *[Brno hlavní nádraží](/wiki/Brno_hlavn%C3%AD_n%C3%A1dra%C5%BE%C3%AD "Brno hlavní nádraží")*, the main railway station, sees about 50,000 passengers and 500 trains every day, and is the hub of regional train travel.[[205]](#cite_note-bmrs-207) The historic station building is outdated and has insufficient operating capacity.[[205]](#cite_note-bmrs-207) A [referendum](/wiki/Brno_central_station_referendum,_2016 "Brno central station referendum, 2016") on the future location of the station was held twice, first in 2004 and again in 2016.[[206]](#cite_note-208)[[207]](#cite_note-209) Although an overwhelming majority of residents wanted the main train station to remain in its current location, the referendum results were not binding due to low turnout.[[208]](#cite_note-210) In 2024, the Czech Ministry of Transport approved the construction of a new main railway station on the site of the current *[Brno dolní nádraží](/w/index.php?title=Brno_doln%C3%AD_n%C3%A1dra%C5%BE%C3%AD&action=edit&redlink=1 "Brno dolní nádraží (page does not exist)") [[cs](https://cs.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brno_doln%C3%AD_n%C3%A1dra%C5%BE%C3%AD "cs:Brno dolní nádraží")]* station. The project is scheduled for completion in 2035.[[209]](#cite_note-211)[[210]](#cite_note-212)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/Brno_departure_terminal.jpg/330px-Brno_departure_terminal.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brno_departure_terminal.jpg)

[Brno-Tuřany](/wiki/Brno-Tu%C5%99any "Brno-Tuřany") Airport is the second busiest airport in the Czech Republic[[211]](#cite_note-213)

Two [motorways](/wiki/Motorway "Motorway"), the [D1](/wiki/D1_motorway_(Czech_Republic) "D1 motorway (Czech Republic)") and the [D2](/wiki/D2_motorway_(Czech_Republic) "D2 motorway (Czech Republic)"), traverse the southern edge of the city. The D1 connects Brno with [Prague](/wiki/Prague "Prague") and [Ostrava](/wiki/Ostrava "Ostrava"), while the D2 connects the city to [Bratislava](/wiki/Bratislava "Bratislava").[[212]](#cite_note-highways-214) The D1 and D2 junction is one of the busiest junctions in the Czech Republic, with up to 80,000 vehicles passing through daily.[[213]](#cite_note-215) To the south of the city limits is the [D52](/wiki/D52_motorway "D52 motorway"), which leads to [Vienna](/wiki/Vienna "Vienna"). Another planned motorway, the [D43](/wiki/D43_motorway_(Czech_Republic) "D43 motorway (Czech Republic)"), will connect Brno to northwestern Moravia.[[214]](#cite_note-216) A [motorway ring](/wiki/Ring_road "Ring road"), [I/42](/w/index.php?title=I/42&action=edit&redlink=1 "I/42 (page does not exist)") [[cs](https://cs.wikipedia.org/wiki/Silnice_I/42 "cs:Silnice I/42")], is under construction to divert traffic around the city.[[215]](#cite_note-217)[[216]](#cite_note-218) Several road tunnels have been built, in Pisárky, Husovice, Hlinky, and Královo Pole, as part of the I/42 project, with more planned.[[217]](#cite_note-219)[[218]](#cite_note-220) Brno uses a zone-based parking system, which offers free parking to residents and charges visitors a fee. The historic city centre is subject to more restrictions.[[219]](#cite_note-221) Additional [car parks](/wiki/Parking_ramp "Parking ramp"), including underground, are being built to alleviate congestion.[[220]](#cite_note-222) In 2024, there were approximately 598 cars per 1,000 residents in Brno; this figure rises by approximately nine vehicles per year.[[195]](#cite_note-:3-197) Brno uses a traffic management system that integrates real-time data and prioritizes public transit to reduce congestion and air emissions.[[221]](#cite_note-223)

[![Velonet bike rentals in front of the Constitutional Court in Brno, Czech Republic](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/16/Brno%2C_Jo%C5%A1tova%2C_kola_Velonet_p%C5%99ed_%C3%9Astavn%C3%ADm_soudem_%281787%29.jpg/250px-Brno%2C_Jo%C5%A1tova%2C_kola_Velonet_p%C5%99ed_%C3%9Astavn%C3%ADm_soudem_%281787%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brno,_Jo%C5%A1tova,_kola_Velonet_p%C5%99ed_%C3%9Astavn%C3%ADm_soudem_(1787).jpg)

Velonet bike rentals in front of the [Constitutional Court](/wiki/Constitutional_Court_of_the_Czech_Republic "Constitutional Court of the Czech Republic")

[Brno-Tuřany Airport](/wiki/Brno-Tu%C5%99any_Airport "Brno-Tuřany Airport") is an international airport located about 7.5 km (5 mi) southeast of Brno's city centre. In 2024, it recorded a passenger volume of 749,153 and a cargo throughput of 11,803 tonnes (26,021,000 lb), a slight increase over the previous year.[[222]](#cite_note-224)[[223]](#cite_note-225)[[224]](#cite_note-ans.cz-226) [Ryanair](/wiki/Ryanair "Ryanair") and other airlines including [Smartwings](/wiki/Smartwings "Smartwings"), [AeroItalia](/wiki/AeroItalia "AeroItalia"), [Aegean](/wiki/Aegean_Airlines "Aegean Airlines") and [Air Montenegro](/wiki/Air_Montenegro "Air Montenegro"), operate scheduled services to European destinations; however, the airport primarily serves seasonal charter flights.[[225]](#cite_note-227)[[226]](#cite_note-228) Brno-Tuřany also serves as a police helicopter base, the only such facility outside of Prague.[[227]](#cite_note-229) Separately, the municipal [Medlánky Airport](/wiki/Medl%C3%A1nky_Airport "Medlánky Airport"), located to the north, serves as a hub for ultralight general aviation and recreational activities, including [hot air balloon flying](/wiki/Hot_air_balloon "Hot air balloon"), [gliding](/wiki/Glider_aircraft "Glider aircraft"), and [model aircraft](/wiki/Radio-controlled_model "Radio-controlled model").[[228]](#cite_note-230)[[229]](#cite_note-231) The airport is home to Aeroklub Brno-Medlánky, which also operates the facility.[[230]](#cite_note-232)[[231]](#cite_note-233)

Cycling is a popular and widespread activity in Brno, due to the city's favorable topography. Despite Brno's car-centric layout and limited availability of bike lanes, 4% of the city's residents use bicycles as a means of transportation.[[232]](#cite_note-ecf.com-234)[[233]](#cite_note-235)[[234]](#cite_note-236) There are seven well-maintained bicycle paths, including those along the Svratka and Svitava rivers, totaling 114 kilometres (71 mi). Regional bike routes include a 76 km (47 mi) [bikeway](/wiki/Bikeway "Bikeway") to [Vienna](/wiki/Vienna "Vienna").[[235]](#cite_note-237) Additionally, several hiking trails maintained by the [Czech Tourist Club](/wiki/Czech_Tourist_Club "Czech Tourist Club") (KČT) pass through or near Brno, including the [Stezka Českem](/w/index.php?title=Stezka_%C4%8Ceskem&action=edit&redlink=1 "Stezka Českem (page does not exist)") [[cs](https://cs.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stezka_%C4%8Ceskem "cs:Stezka Českem")] national trail and numerous local marked trails.[[236]](#cite_note-238)[[237]](#cite_note-239)[[238]](#cite_note-240) As of 2025, two [bicycle-sharing companies](/wiki/List_of_bicycle-sharing_systems "List of bicycle-sharing systems") operated in Brno: [Nextbike](/wiki/Nextbike "Nextbike") and [Rekola](/wiki/Rekola "Rekola").[[239]](#cite_note-:0-241) [Lime](/wiki/Lime_(transportation_company) "Lime (transportation company)") is the sole operator of shared rental scooters.[[240]](#cite_note-242) In 2024, approximately 16,000 residents used bike-sharing services for 160,000 journeys, covering a distance of 276,000 kilometres (171,000 mi).[[232]](#cite_note-ecf.com-234)[[239]](#cite_note-:0-241)

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brno&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Culture")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ea/Lisenske_slavnosti_vyrez.jpg/250px-Lisenske_slavnosti_vyrez.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lisenske_slavnosti_vyrez.jpg)

People wearing the traditional Moravian *[kroje](/wiki/Kroj "Kroj")* (Moravian national folk costumes) at a folk festival in Líšeň

In 2023, the city spent approximately CZK 1.73 billion (€71 million) on culture. A university city with approximately 65,000 students, Brno is home to numerous museums, theatres, and other cultural institutions, and hosts a variety of festivals and cultural events. On average, Brno spent CZK 3,878.5 (€160) per capita on culture annually, significantly ahead of cities such as [Ostrava](/wiki/Ostrava "Ostrava") (CZK 3,137.74), [Olomouc](/wiki/Olomouc "Olomouc") (CZK 2,812.07), and [Prague](/wiki/Prague "Prague") (CZK 1,790.51).[[241]](#cite_note-243) Brno features a rich cultural life, with a diverse range of institutions and artists. More than 11,000 entities and approximately 21,000 residents (comprising about 12% of Brno's economy) are engaged in creative industries and culture. The estimated annual turnover of the entire sector exceeds CZK 24 billion (€990 million).[[242]](#cite_note-244)

Brno has experienced a significant cultural "rebirth" since the [Velvet Revolution](/wiki/Velvet_Revolution "Velvet Revolution") of 1989. The façades of historical monuments have been repaired, and various exhibitions, shows, and other events are being established or expanded. In 2007, a summit of 15 presidents of EU Member States was held in Brno.[[243]](#cite_note-245) Despite its urban character, some of the city's districts still preserve traditional Moravian folklore, including folk festivals featuring traditional Moravian costumes, local wines, folk music, and dances, held in several city districts, including [Židenice](/wiki/Brno-%C5%BDidenice "Brno-Židenice"), [Líšeň](/wiki/Brno-L%C3%AD%C5%A1e%C5%88 "Brno-Líšeň"), and [Ivanovice](/wiki/Brno-Ivanovice "Brno-Ivanovice").[[244]](#cite_note-246)[[245]](#cite_note-247)[[246]](#cite_note-248) A local dialect, [Hantec](/wiki/Hantec "Hantec"), originated in Brno. While the dialect itself is no longer in common use, certain words are still used,[[247]](#cite_note-249) such as *šalina* (the common local term for a tram), *štatl*, (the city centre), and *rola*, (a railway station).[[248]](#cite_note-250)

### Festivals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brno&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Festivals")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/Ignis_Brunensis_2010_Montage_1.jpg/250px-Ignis_Brunensis_2010_Montage_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ignis_Brunensis_2010_Montage_1.jpg)

Ignis Brunensis fireworks festival on the [Brno Reservoir](/wiki/Brno_Reservoir "Brno Reservoir") (2010)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9b/Tram_horse_tram_Brno.jpg/250px-Tram_horse_tram_Brno.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tram_horse_tram_Brno.jpg)

Historical [horse-drawn tram](/wiki/Horse-drawn_tram "Horse-drawn tram") at the festival called "Brno – City in the Centre of Europe"

The largest festival in Brno is the Ignis Brunensis (Latin for "Flame of Brno") fireworks competition and drone show, held annually in June as part of the "Brno – City in the Centre of Europe" festival.[[249]](#cite_note-251) Ignis Brunensis is the largest event of its kind in Central Europe, with over 1.3 million attendees in 2014.[[250]](#cite_note-252)[[251]](#cite_note-253)[[16]](#cite_note-:9-16) *[Cinema Mundi](/wiki/Cinema_Mundi_International_Film_Festival "Cinema Mundi International Film Festival")* was an international film festival held annually at the end of winter between 2010 and 2016. Approximately 60 films competed for an [Oscar](/wiki/Academy_Award "Academy Award") nomination in the category of Best Foreign Language Film.[[252]](#cite_note-254) It resumed in 2024 with its 8th edition.[[253]](#cite_note-255) *Theatre World Brno* is one of the largest theatre festivals in the Czech Republic. It is a collaboration between the [National Theatre Brno](/wiki/National_Theatre_Brno "National Theatre Brno"), [Husa na provázku Theatre](/w/index.php?title=Husa_na_prov%C3%A1zku_Theatre&action=edit&redlink=1 "Husa na provázku Theatre (page does not exist)") [[cs](https://cs.wikipedia.org/wiki/Husa_na_prov%C3%A1zku "cs:Husa na provázku")], Municipal Theatre Brno, JAMU, and several other venues. It features dozens of performances by national and international ensembles.[[254]](#cite_note-256)[[255]](#cite_note-257)

Other festivals held regularly in Brno include the Spilberk International Music Festival and the Summer Shakespeare Festival.[[256]](#cite_note-258)[[257]](#cite_note-259)[[258]](#cite_note-260) Every September, Brno hosts the *Slavnosti vína* wine festival to celebrate the harvest in the surrounding wine-producing region.[[259]](#cite_note-261) The Moravian Autumn festival, established as the Brno International Music Festival in 1966, is among the highest-profile cultural events in the Czech Republic, bringing leading international orchestras, chamber ensembles and soloists to Brno.[[260]](#cite_note-262)[[261]](#cite_note-263) The Janáček Brno festival is an international opera and music festival that celebrates the works of Leoš Janáček and other Czech composers, including Antonín Dvořák. Performances are often televised and broadcast on the radio.[[262]](#cite_note-264)[[263]](#cite_note-265)

Brno also hosts historical reenactments and commemorative events. The reenactment of the [Battle of Austerlitz](/wiki/Battle_of_Austerlitz "Battle of Austerlitz") draws over 1,000 attendees from more than a dozen countries each year.[[264]](#cite_note-266) Another historic reenactment and parade is the Day of Brno, which commemorates the day when Brno successfully repelled the Swedish army during the Thirty Years' War. It features a re-enacted battle at the Špilberk Castle and a parade in historical uniforms through the city centre.[[265]](#cite_note-267) The Annual Pilgrimage of Reconciliation commemorates the [expulsion of German residents](/wiki/Expulsion_of_Germans_from_Czechoslovakia "Expulsion of Germans from Czechoslovakia") and the so-called [Brno Death March](/wiki/Brno_death_march "Brno death march"). The Reconciliation March is now part of the Meeting Brno festival.[[266]](#cite_note-268)[[267]](#cite_note-269) It was first held in 2015 and has grown significantly in size, with 13,000 attendees in 2023.[[268]](#cite_note-270) In addition to the Brno International Folklore Festival, several Brno districts, such as [Medlánky](/wiki/Brno-Medl%C3%A1nky "Brno-Medlánky") and [Černovice](/wiki/Brno-%C4%8Cernovice "Brno-Černovice"), host their own *[hody](/wiki/Hody_(Moravia) "Hody (Moravia)")*. This traditional Moravian village celebration combines local customs and festive activities. They are usually held in the summer and early fall, and feature folk costumes (Czech: *[kroj](/wiki/Kroj "Kroj")*), traditional music, dancing, and a maypole.[[269]](#cite_note-271)[[270]](#cite_note-272)[[271]](#cite_note-273)

### Theatres

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brno&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Theatres")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/78/Reduta_Theatre_-_Brno.jpg/250px-Reduta_Theatre_-_Brno.jpg)](/wiki/File:Reduta_Theatre_-_Brno.jpg)

[Reduta Theatre](/wiki/Reduta_Theatre "Reduta Theatre"), the oldest theatre in Central Europe

Brno features the oldest theatre building in Central Europe, the [Reduta Theatre](/wiki/Reduta_Theatre "Reduta Theatre"), located on [Zelný trh](/wiki/Zeln%C3%BD_trh "Zelný trh"), which bears witness to the city's long tradition of the performing arts. The first theatre plays in Brno likely took place in the 1660s at what was the *City Tavern*, an early precursor to the Reduta Theatre. However, the first theatre building with [opera boxes](/wiki/Box_(theatre) "Box (theatre)") was constructed later, in 1733. The first documented theatre performance in the Czech language took place here in 1767. The play was called *Zamilovaný ponocný* (English: Watchman in Love) and was performed by the Baden Theatre Company. [Mozart](/wiki/Wolfgang_Amadeus_Mozart "Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart") performed at the theatre with his sister Anna Maria ([Nannerl](/wiki/Nannerl "Nannerl")) while his family spent Christmas in Brno that year.[[272]](#cite_note-redutatheatre-274)[[273]](#cite_note-275)[[274]](#cite_note-276) A Mozart statue stands in front of the theatre, commemorating their visit, and the theatre's *Mozart Hall* is named in his honor.[[275]](#cite_note-277)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/Mesto_Brno_-_Mahenovo_divadlo_2.jpg/250px-Mesto_Brno_-_Mahenovo_divadlo_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mesto_Brno_-_Mahenovo_divadlo_2.jpg)

[Mahen Theatre](/wiki/Mahen_Theatre "Mahen Theatre")

The [National Theatre Brno](/wiki/National_Theatre_Brno "National Theatre Brno") (NdB) is the leading producer of [opera](/wiki/Opera "Opera"), [drama](/wiki/Drama "Drama"), and [ballet](/wiki/Ballet "Ballet") in Brno.[[276]](#cite_note-278)[[277]](#cite_note-279)[[278]](#cite_note-280) NdB's first permanent presence was established in 1884. Today, the [National Theatre Brno](/wiki/National_Theatre_Brno "National Theatre Brno") also owns the [Mahen Theatre](/wiki/Mahen_Theatre "Mahen Theatre"), which was built in 1882, the [Janáček Theatre](/wiki/Jan%C3%A1%C4%8Dek_Theatre "Janáček Theatre"), built in 1965, and the [Reduta Theatre](/wiki/Reduta_Theatre "Reduta Theatre").[[279]](#cite_note-281) The composer [Leoš Janáček](/wiki/Leo%C5%A1_Jan%C3%A1%C4%8Dek "Leoš Janáček") is also associated with the National Theatre Brno. The Mahen Theatre was the first theatre building in Europe illuminated by [Thomas Edison's](/wiki/Thomas_Edison "Thomas Edison") [electric light bulbs](/wiki/Electric_light_bulb "Electric light bulb"), which were a novelty at the time. A small steam-driven electric power plant was constructed near the theatre before Edison's visit to Brno in 1911.[[280]](#cite_note-mahentheatre-282) The [Brno City Theatre](/wiki/Brno_City_Theatre "Brno City Theatre"), founded in 1945, is the most commercially successful ensemble in Brno.[[281]](#cite_note-283) The theatre's local performances are routinely sold out. It performs abroad as well.[[282]](#cite_note-284) The theatre's repertoire consists primarily of musical and dramatic shows.[[283]](#cite_note-285)

There is a variety of smaller theatre venues in Brno, including the [Bolek Polivka](/wiki/Bolek_Polivka "Bolek Polivka") theatre, the [Husa na provázku Theatre](/w/index.php?title=Husa_na_prov%C3%A1zku_Theatre&action=edit&redlink=1 "Husa na provázku Theatre (page does not exist)") [[cs](https://cs.wikipedia.org/wiki/Husa_na_prov%C3%A1zku "cs:Husa na provázku")], [HaDivadlo](/w/index.php?title=HaDivadlo&action=edit&redlink=1 "HaDivadlo (page does not exist)") [[cs](https://cs.wikipedia.org/wiki/HaDivadlo "cs:HaDivadlo")], [Divadlo Radost](/w/index.php?title=Divadlo_Radost&action=edit&redlink=1 "Divadlo Radost (page does not exist)") [[cs](https://cs.wikipedia.org/wiki/Divadlo_Radost "cs:Divadlo Radost")], and [Divadlo Polárka](/w/index.php?title=Divadlo_Pol%C3%A1rka&action=edit&redlink=1 "Divadlo Polárka (page does not exist)") [[cs](https://cs.wikipedia.org/wiki/Divadlo_Pol%C3%A1rka "cs:Divadlo Polárka")]. The Mahen Theatre was initially known as the City Theatre and, until 1918, hosted performances exclusively in German; it was not part of the National Theatre of Brno at that time. Between 1971 and 1978, some plays were hosted by the [Brno Exhibition Centre](/wiki/Brno_Exhibition_Centre "Brno Exhibition Centre") while the Mahen Theatre underwent reconstruction.[[284]](#cite_note-286)

### Local legends

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brno&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Local legends")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cd/Brn%C4%9Bnsk%C3%A9_kolo_a_drak_%2802%29_v%C3%BD%C5%99ez.jpg/250px-Brn%C4%9Bnsk%C3%A9_kolo_a_drak_%2802%29_v%C3%BD%C5%99ez.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brn%C4%9Bnsk%C3%A9_kolo_a_drak_(02)_v%C3%BD%C5%99ez.jpg)

Brno Dragon and Brno Wheel at the Old Town Hall

Alligators, crocodiles, and dragons are referenced heavily in Brno, due to a local legend dating back to the Middle Ages. According to one version of the legend, a frightening creature terrorized the residents of Brno. Unfamiliar with a crocodile, they referred to it as a dragon. They lived in fear until a young butcher devised a plan to kill the monster. He filled a cowhide with quicklime and sewed it up. After eating this, the crocodile attempted to wash down the bitter aftertaste with water until its stomach burst and it died. According to another version of the story, Margrave Matthias (later the [Holy Roman Emperor](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Emperor "Holy Roman Emperor")) was fond of animals, and the Ottomans gifted him a live crocodile. As Matthias passed through the streets of Brno, the crocodile died, and the Margrave left it behind in the city, where it has been on display ever since at the [Brno Old Town Hall](/w/index.php?title=Brno_Old_Town_Hall&action=edit&redlink=1 "Brno Old Town Hall (page does not exist)") [[cs](https://cs.wikipedia.org/wiki/Star%C3%A1_radnice_(Brno) "cs:Stará radnice (Brno)")].[[285]](#cite_note-:10-287)

Over the years, the creature became one of the mascots of Brno. A stuffed baguette is called a crocodile ([Czech](/wiki/Czech_language "Czech language"): *krokodýl*), as is the radio station, known as Radio Krokodýl. One of the local [baseball](/wiki/Baseball "Baseball") teams is named [Draci Brno](/wiki/Draci_Brno "Draci Brno") (English: Brno Dragons), the rugby club is [RC Dragon Brno](/wiki/RC_Dragon_Brno "RC Dragon Brno"), and the [American football](/wiki/American_football "American football") team goes by [Brno Alligators](/w/index.php?title=Brno_Alligators&action=edit&redlink=1 "Brno Alligators (page does not exist)") [[cs](https://cs.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brno_Alligators "cs:Brno Alligators")]. An [Intercity](/wiki/Inter-city_rail "Inter-city rail") train connection between Brno and Prague is named *Brněnský drak* (English: Brno dragon). The 2024 Christmas season featured a dragon clothed in a neon jumper spewing flames. The particular specimen on display in the Old Town Hall passage is a female Nile crocodile of unknown age, about 5 m (16 ft) in length, and weighing 200 kg (440 lb). Its skin has been reinforced with metal and a plaster cast.[[285]](#cite_note-:10-287)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/Brno-AltesRathaus2.jpg/250px-Brno-AltesRathaus2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brno-AltesRathaus2.jpg)

Old Town Hall in Brno

Next to the crocodile is a wagon wheel, another symbol of the city. According to the legend, a man made a wager that he could fell a tree in [Lednice](/wiki/Lednice "Lednice"), 50 km (31 mi) from the city, craft a wheel out of it, and roll with it to town all within a single day. Since the feat was deemed to be impossible, the man was believed to have made a pact with the devil, was ostracized, and died penniless as a result.[[285]](#cite_note-:10-287)

Another local legend dates back to the [siege of the city](/wiki/Siege_of_Brno "Siege of Brno") by the Swedish army during the [Thirty Years' War](/wiki/Thirty_Years%27_War "Thirty Years' War") in 1645. The standoff was at a stalemate, and the Swedish marshal [Lennart Torstensson](/wiki/Lennart_Torstensson "Lennart Torstensson") declared that he would withdraw if the city did not surrender to him by noon. The bell ringer at the [Cathedral of Saints Peter and Paul](/wiki/Cathedral_of_St._Peter_and_Paul,_Brno "Cathedral of St. Peter and Paul, Brno") on Petrov Hill tricked the Swedes by sounding noon an hour early. In keeping his word, Field Marshal Torstensson and his army left.[[286]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHenig200093-288) As a historic tribute to the Swedish siege, the bell at the Cathedral of Saints Peter and Paul still rings noon an hour early, at 11 o'clock. The [Brno astronomical clock](/wiki/Brno_astronomical_clock "Brno astronomical clock") releases a glass ball at 11 a.m. every day to commemorate the heroic defense of the city. On special occasions, a glass ball rolls out every hour. As the hour approaches, the clock is typically surrounded by throngs of people hoping to catch one of the glass balls as a souvenir.[[287]](#cite_note-289)

### Museums, libraries and galleries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brno&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Museums, libraries and galleries")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/Moravsk%C3%A1_zemsk%C3%A1_knihovna_crop.jpg/250px-Moravsk%C3%A1_zemsk%C3%A1_knihovna_crop.jpg)](/wiki/File:Moravsk%C3%A1_zemsk%C3%A1_knihovna_crop.jpg)

Moravian Library building

The most significant museum in Brno is the [Moravian Museum](/wiki/Moravian_Museum "Moravian Museum"), the largest in Moravia and the second-largest in the Czech Republic.[[288]](#cite_note-mzm-290) The museum was founded in 1817, and its collections comprise over 6 million pieces.[[288]](#cite_note-mzm-290) The most extensive public library in Brno is the [Moravian Library](/wiki/Moravian_Library "Moravian Library"). With approximately 4 million volumes, it is the second-largest library in the Czech Republic.[[289]](#cite_note-291) The most extensive gallery in Brno is the [Moravian Gallery](/wiki/Moravian_Gallery "Moravian Gallery"), which again ranks second in the Czech Republic and is the largest in Moravia.[[290]](#cite_note-292)

One section of the Moravian Museum, the [Anthropos Pavilion](/wiki/Anthropos_Pavilion "Anthropos Pavilion"), is related to the earliest history of humanity and prehistoric Europe. Brno is also home to one of the largest technical museums in the Czech Republic, which hosts 17 permanent displays charting the advance of science and technology, accompanied by various models and restored machinery.[[291]](#cite_note-Technical_Museum-293) The museum also hosts multiple short-term exhibitions that complement its emphasis on metallurgy, cutlery, mechanical music, steamships, clockwork, and water machines.[[292]](#cite_note-294)

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brno&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Education")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/Kampus_2_%28Fotoarchiv_RMU%29.jpg/250px-Kampus_2_%28Fotoarchiv_RMU%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kampus_2_(Fotoarchiv_RMU).jpg)

Faculty of Education, [Masaryk University](/wiki/Masaryk_University "Masaryk University"), [Brno-Bohunice](/wiki/Brno-Bohunice "Brno-Bohunice")

Brno is home to 10 universities and more than 60 secondary schools.[[8]](#cite_note-:7-8)[[9]](#cite_note-:6-9)[[10]](#cite_note-:8-10) In 2024, over 65,000 students were enrolled in Brno's higher education institutions.[[9]](#cite_note-:6-9) The student population has increased by 6.4% over the past five years, reversing the downward trend that had persisted through the 2010s, and now one in six Brno residents is a student.[[293]](#cite_note-295) The city is also home to several [research and development](/wiki/Research_and_development "Research and development") institutions, including the [Central European Institute of Technology](/wiki/Central_European_Institute_of_Technology "Central European Institute of Technology") (CEITEC) and the [International Clinical Research Center](/wiki/International_Clinical_Research_Center "International Clinical Research Center") (ICRC).[[294]](#cite_note-296)[[295]](#cite_note-297) While classes taught in English are subject to tuition fees, instruction in Czech is free to everyone, including international students. The three most prominent universities in the region, [Masaryk University](/wiki/Masaryk_University "Masaryk University"), [Brno University of Technology](/wiki/Brno_University_of_Technology "Brno University of Technology"), and [Mendel University in Brno](/wiki/Mendel_University_in_Brno "Mendel University in Brno"), all participate in the Erasmus programme.[[296]](#cite_note-298)

With over 35,000 students, [Masaryk University](/wiki/Masaryk_University "Masaryk University"), also known as MUNI, is the largest university in Brno. Founded in 1919, it comprises ten faculties, 400 study programmes, over 190 departments, institutes, and clinics.[[297]](#cite_note-299)[[298]](#cite_note-300)[[299]](#cite_note-301) MUNI consistently ranks among the top 500 universities globally and second, after [Charles University](/wiki/Charles_University "Charles University"), in the Czech Republic.[[300]](#cite_note-302)[[301]](#cite_note-303)[[302]](#cite_note-304) The [Brno University of Technology](/wiki/Brno_University_of_Technology "Brno University of Technology"), VUT, was established in 1899 and is now one of the largest technical universities in the Czech Republic, with approximately 18,000 students. It ranks among the top 41% of universities globally and fifth in the nation.[[303]](#cite_note-305) [Viktor Kaplan](/wiki/Viktor_Kaplan "Viktor Kaplan"), the inventor of the [Kaplan turbine](/wiki/Kaplan_turbine "Kaplan turbine"), spent nearly 30 years at the [German Technical University in Brno](/wiki/German_Technical_University_in_Brno "German Technical University in Brno"), a precursor institution that closed after World War II in 1945.[[304]](#cite_note-306)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/da/Zem%C4%9Bd%C4%9Blsk%C3%A1_1%2C_Brno.JPG/250px-Zem%C4%9Bd%C4%9Blsk%C3%A1_1%2C_Brno.JPG)](/wiki/File:Zem%C4%9Bd%C4%9Blsk%C3%A1_1,_Brno.JPG)

[Mendel University in Brno](/wiki/Mendel_University_in_Brno "Mendel University in Brno")

The [Mendel University](/wiki/Mendel_University_in_Brno "Mendel University in Brno"), or MENDELU, was established in 1919 and is named after [Gregor Mendel](/wiki/Gregor_Mendel "Gregor Mendel"), the founder of genetics, who developed his [revolutionary scientific theories](/wiki/Mendelian_inheritance "Mendelian inheritance") in Brno. It focuses on life sciences and has over 10,000 students.[[305]](#cite_note-307)[[306]](#cite_note-308) It ranks 9th in the Czech Republic and 1267th globally (177th in Plant and Animal Science).[[307]](#cite_note-309)

The [Janáček Academy of Music and Performing Arts](/wiki/Jan%C3%A1%C4%8Dek_Academy_of_Music_and_Performing_Arts "Janáček Academy of Music and Performing Arts"), or JAMU, named after [Leoš Janáček](/wiki/Leo%C5%A1_Jan%C3%A1%C4%8Dek "Leoš Janáček"), was founded in 1947 and is one of two academies of music and drama in the Czech Republic.[[308]](#cite_note-310)[[309]](#cite_note-311)[[310]](#cite_note-312) It holds the annual [Leoš Janáček Competition](/wiki/Leo%C5%A1_Jan%C3%A1%C4%8Dek_Competition "Leoš Janáček Competition").[[311]](#cite_note-jamu2-313) The [University of Veterinary Sciences Brno](/wiki/University_of_Veterinary_Sciences_Brno "University of Veterinary Sciences Brno") is the only university in the Czech Republic for the study of veterinary sciences. Founded in 1918, the VETUNI has an estimated total enrollment of approximately 350 students, with 76 new students enrolled in the 2024/25 academic year.[[312]](#cite_note-314) VETUNI ranks 699th in Plant and Animal Science globally.[[313]](#cite_note-315)

## Sport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brno&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Sport")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/Start_Superbike_Weltmeisterschaft_2008_Brno.JPG/250px-Start_Superbike_Weltmeisterschaft_2008_Brno.JPG)](/wiki/File:Start_Superbike_Weltmeisterschaft_2008_Brno.JPG)

[Motorcycle racing](/wiki/Motorcycle_racing "Motorcycle racing") championship at the [Masaryk Circuit](/wiki/Masaryk_Circuit "Masaryk Circuit")

The city has a long association with [motor racing](/wiki/Motorsport "Motorsport"); among other events, the [Masaryk Circuit](/wiki/Brno_Circuit "Brno Circuit") has hosted the [Moto GP](/wiki/Grand_Prix_motorcycle_racing "Grand Prix motorcycle racing") championship since 1965. The annual [Czech Republic motorcycle Grand Prix](/wiki/Czech_Republic_motorcycle_Grand_Prix "Czech Republic motorcycle Grand Prix"), the most famous motor race in the Czech Republic, has been held in the city since 1950. Since 1968, Brno has been a permanent fixture on the [European Touring Car Championship](/wiki/European_Touring_Car_Championship "European Touring Car Championship") (ETCC) series.[[314]](#cite_note-316)

[Track](/wiki/Track_cycling "Track cycling") and [road cycling](/wiki/Road_bicycle_racing "Road bicycle racing") have a long history in Brno. The first track races took place here in 1889, and the [velodrome](/wiki/Velodrome "Velodrome") in Brno ranks among the [oldest velodromes](/wiki/List_of_cycling_tracks_and_velodromes#List_of_oldest_cycling_tracks_and_velodromes "List of cycling tracks and velodromes") in the world. In 1969, Brno hosted the [UCI Track Cycling World](/wiki/1969_UCI_Track_Cycling_World_Championships "1969 UCI Track Cycling World Championships") Championships and the [UCI Road World Championships](/wiki/1969_UCI_Road_World_Championships "1969 UCI Road World Championships") for amateurs. In 1981, the [UCI Track Cycling World Championships](/wiki/1981_UCI_Track_Cycling_World_Championships "1981 UCI Track Cycling World Championships") were held, and in 2001, the [UEC European Track Championships](/wiki/2001_UEC_European_Track_Championships "2001 UEC European Track Championships") were held for the U23 category. There are two traditional cycling teams: [TJ Favorit Brno](http://www.favoritbrno.cz/) and [Dukla Brno](https://www.duklabrnosprint.cz/).

The [2010 FIBA World Championship for Women](/wiki/2010_FIBA_World_Championship_for_Women "2010 FIBA World Championship for Women") was played in Brno's [Arena Vodova](/wiki/Arena_Vodova "Arena Vodova"), with the Czech squad taking the silver medal.

There is a horse-race course at Brno-Dvorská and an aeroclub airport in [Medlánky Airport](/wiki/Medl%C3%A1nky_Airport "Medlánky Airport"). Several sports clubs represent the city in the various Czech leagues, including [FC Zbrojovka Brno](/wiki/FC_Zbrojovka_Brno "FC Zbrojovka Brno") ([football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football")), [HC Kometa Brno](/wiki/HC_Kometa_Brno "HC Kometa Brno") ([ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey")), [KP Brno](/w/index.php?title=KP_Brno&action=edit&redlink=1 "KP Brno (page does not exist)") [[cs](https://cs.wikipedia.org/wiki/KP_Brno_(basketbal) "cs:KP Brno (basketbal)")] ([basketball](/wiki/Basketball "Basketball"), women),[[315]](#cite_note-317) [BC Brno](/wiki/BC_Brno "BC Brno") ([basketball](/wiki/Basketball "Basketball"), men) and [BK Brno](/wiki/BK_Brno "BK Brno") (women), four [baseball](/wiki/Baseball "Baseball") teams ([Draci Brno](/wiki/Draci_Brno "Draci Brno"), Hroši Brno, VSK Technika Brno, MZLU Express Brno), [Brno Ravens LC](/w/index.php?title=Brno_Ravens_LC&action=edit&redlink=1 "Brno Ravens LC (page does not exist)") [[cs](https://cs.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brno_Ravens_LC "cs:Brno Ravens LC")] (lacrosse club), Brno Alligators ([American football](/wiki/American_football "American football")), two [rugby](/wiki/Rugby_football "Rugby football") teams ([RC Dragon Brno](/wiki/RC_Dragon_Brno "RC Dragon Brno"), RC Bystrc) and others. [Tennis](/wiki/Tennis "Tennis") players [Barbora Krejčíková](/wiki/Barbora_Krej%C4%8D%C3%ADkov%C3%A1 "Barbora Krejčíková"), [Lucie Šafářová](/wiki/Lucie_%C5%A0af%C3%A1%C5%99ov%C3%A1 "Lucie Šafářová"), [Lukáš Rosol](/wiki/Luk%C3%A1%C5%A1_Rosol "Lukáš Rosol"), and [Jana Novotná](/wiki/Jana_Novotn%C3%A1 "Jana Novotná") are from Brno, as well as [Michal Březina](/wiki/Michal_B%C5%99ezina "Michal Březina"), one of the top Czech figure skaters.

[Motorcycle speedway](/wiki/Motorcycle_speedway "Motorcycle speedway") events were held at the Winter Stadium, off Na Rybníčku in Králové Pole.[[316]](#cite_note-318) The venue hosted a final round of the [Czechoslovak Individual Speedway Championship](/wiki/Czech_Republic_Individual_Speedway_Championship "Czech Republic Individual Speedway Championship") in 1949, 1950 and 1954.[[317]](#cite_note-319)

## Sights

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brno&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Sights")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/Brno_-_Cathedral_of_Saints_Peter_and_Paul_II.jpg/250px-Brno_-_Cathedral_of_Saints_Peter_and_Paul_II.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brno_-_Cathedral_of_Saints_Peter_and_Paul_II.jpg)

The Cathedral of Saints Peter and Paul and Dietrichstein Palace viewed from the tower of the Old Town Hall

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ab/Tugendhat_zevnitr_vyrez.jpg/250px-Tugendhat_zevnitr_vyrez.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tugendhat_zevnitr_vyrez.jpg)

Villa Tugendhat, protected as a UNESCO World Heritage Site

Brno has over 400 protected cultural sites and objects, including one designated as a [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") [World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site"), and 14 monuments on the [List of national cultural monuments of the Czech Republic](/w/index.php?title=List_of_national_cultural_monuments_of_the_Czech_Republic&action=edit&redlink=1 "List of national cultural monuments of the Czech Republic (page does not exist)") [[cs](https://cs.wikipedia.org/wiki/Seznam_n%C3%A1rodn%C3%ADch_kulturn%C3%ADch_pam%C3%A1tek_%C4%8Cesk%C3%A9_republiky "cs:Seznam národních kulturních památek České republiky")].[[318]](#cite_note-320)[[319]](#cite_note-unesco-321)[[320]](#cite_note-322) Most of them are in the historical city centre, but some are located in the outer districts of the city.

The Old Town Hall (Czech: *Stará radnice*), dating back to approximately 1240, is the oldest secular building in Brno and has been registered as a national cultural monument since 2024. Its observation deck is 63 metres above ground, and the central passageway houses the Brno dragon and a cartwheel, both symbols of the city.[[321]](#cite_note-323) Špilberk Castle, originally a royal seat founded in the 13th century, became a fortress and later a prison from the 17th century onwards (e.g., the [Carbonari](/wiki/Carbonari "Carbonari")). Today, it is one of the city's major landmarks.[[58]](#cite_note-spilberkhist-59)[[322]](#cite_note-324) The park and gardens around the castle, established in 1861, are notable for their landscaping, as well as an observation point also known as the Špilberk Arbour.[[323]](#cite_note-:12-325)[[324]](#cite_note-326)

Another key landmark is the [Cathedral of St. Peter and Paul](/wiki/Cathedral_of_St._Peter_and_Paul,_Brno "Cathedral of St. Peter and Paul, Brno"), located on Petrov Hill. The site initially housed an 11th-century Romanesque chapel, which was rebuilt into a Romanesque basilica in the 13th century.[[325]](#cite_note-brnopetrov-327) The exterior of the cathedral was rebuilt as a three-nave Gothic basilica in the 14th century. Further modifications followed in the 15th and 16th centuries. The interior is mostly Baroque, as a result of extensive renovations in the mid-18th century.[[326]](#cite_note-328) The structure was completed in its present form, including the addition of two distinct [Neo-Gothic](/wiki/Neo-Gothic "Neo-Gothic") towers designed by architect [August Kirstein](/w/index.php?title=August_Kirstein&action=edit&redlink=1 "August Kirstein (page does not exist)") [[de](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/August_Kirstein "de:August Kirstein")], in 1909.[[327]](#cite_note-329)[[328]](#cite_note-330) The cathedral is a national cultural monument and its image is featured on the Czech 10-koruna coin.[[329]](#cite_note-331)

[Veveří Castle](/wiki/Veve%C5%99%C3%AD_Castle "Veveří Castle"), about 20 km (12 mi) from the city centre, is the third-largest historic monument in Brno.[[19]](#cite_note-veveri-19)[[323]](#cite_note-:12-325) Founded in the Middle Ages as a hunting lodge for the Moravian margraves, it is among the largest and oldest castles in the Czech Republic. Its last owner was Baron [Maurice de Forest](/wiki/Maurice_de_Forest "Maurice de Forest"), an early motor racing driver, aviator, and close friend of [Winston Churchill](/wiki/Winston_Churchill "Winston Churchill"), who visited the castle several times.[[330]](#cite_note-332)[[331]](#cite_note-333)[[332]](#cite_note-334)

The [Abbey of Saint Thomas](/wiki/St_Thomas%27s_Abbey,_Brno "St Thomas's Abbey, Brno"), the site of [Gregor Mendel](/wiki/Gregor_Mendel "Gregor Mendel")'s [genetics](/wiki/Genetics "Genetics") experiments, is also linked with [Leoš Janáček](/wiki/Leo%C5%A1_Jan%C3%A1%C4%8Dek "Leoš Janáček"). The [Church of Saint Tomas](/wiki/Church_of_St._Tomas_(Brno) "Church of St. Tomas (Brno)") houses the graves of [John Henry](/wiki/John_Henry,_Margrave_of_Moravia "John Henry, Margrave of Moravia"), its founder, and his son, [Jobst of Moravia](/wiki/Jobst_of_Moravia "Jobst of Moravia"), both margraves of Moravia. The [Basilica of the Assumption of Our Lady](/wiki/Basilica_of_the_Assumption_of_Our_Lady,_Brno "Basilica of the Assumption of Our Lady, Brno") is the final resting place of Queen [Elisabeth Richeza](/wiki/Elisabeth_Richeza_of_Poland "Elisabeth Richeza of Poland"). The [Church of Saint James](/wiki/Church_of_St._James_(Brno) "Church of St. James (Brno)") is one of the best-preserved [Gothic](/wiki/Gothic_architecture "Gothic architecture") churches in Brno.

The [Brno Ossuary](/wiki/Brno_Ossuary "Brno Ossuary") is the second largest in Europe after the [Catacombs of Paris](/wiki/Catacombs_of_Paris "Catacombs of Paris").[[333]](#cite_note-ossuary-335) Another smaller ossuary is the [Capuchin Crypt](/wiki/Capuchin_Crypt_in_Brno "Capuchin Crypt in Brno"), which houses [mummies](/wiki/Mummy "Mummy") of Capuchin monks and other notable figures of their era, including architect [Mořic Grimm](/w/index.php?title=Mo%C5%99ic_Grimm&action=edit&redlink=1 "Mořic Grimm (page does not exist)") [[cs](https://cs.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mo%C5%99ic_Grimm "cs:Mořic Grimm")] and the mercenary leader [Baron Trenk](/wiki/Baron_Trenk "Baron Trenk").[[334]](#cite_note-mumies-336) The [Labyrinth under Zelný trh](/wiki/Zeln%C3%BD_trh#Labyrinth_under_Zelný_trh "Zelný trh"), a system of underground corridors and cellars dating back to the Middle Ages, have historically served as beer and wine cellars, food storage, and wartime shelters, and have more recently become a tourist attraction. They were previously not as interconnected as they are now, following an extensive reconstruction completed in 2009.[[335]](#cite_note-337)[[336]](#cite_note-338)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/Brno_Parnas_Fountain-02.jpg/250px-Brno_Parnas_Fountain-02.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brno_Parnas_Fountain-02.jpg)

[Zelný trh](/wiki/Zeln%C3%BD_trh "Zelný trh") with Parnassus Fountain

Brno was home to a sizable Jewish community since at least the 13th century. The city is home to a functionalist synagogue built between 1934 and 1936, and the largest [Jewish cemetery](/wiki/Jewish_cemetery "Jewish cemetery") in Moravia.[[337]](#cite_note-zob.cz-339) Tombstones and other cemetery artifacts date back to 1349.[[337]](#cite_note-zob.cz-339) It is estimated that the Jewish community comprised about 12,000 individuals in 1938. Only about 1,000 of them survived the [Holocaust](/wiki/The_Holocaust "The Holocaust") and [Nazi](/wiki/Nazi "Nazi") persecution during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II").[[337]](#cite_note-zob.cz-339) The cemetery and the synagogue are maintained by Brno's remaining Jewish community.[[338]](#cite_note-340) The only [mosque](/wiki/Mosque "Mosque") in the Czech Republic, founded in 1998, is also located in Brno.[[339]](#cite_note-341)

The interwar period saw a significant construction boom, as evidenced by numerous [modernist](/wiki/Modern_architecture "Modern architecture") and [functionalist](/wiki/Functionalism_(architecture) "Functionalism (architecture)") buildings.[[340]](#cite_note-342)[[341]](#cite_note-343) The [Villa Tugendhat](/wiki/Villa_Tugendhat "Villa Tugendhat"), designed by architect [Ludwig Mies van der Rohe](/wiki/Ludwig_Mies_van_der_Rohe "Ludwig Mies van der Rohe") in the 1920s for the family of [Fritz Tugendhat](/w/index.php?title=Fritz_Tugendhat&action=edit&redlink=1 "Fritz Tugendhat (page does not exist)") [[cs](https://cs.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fritz_Tugendhat "cs:Fritz Tugendhat")], was completed in 1930. It was designated a [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") [World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site") in 2001.[[342]](#cite_note-villa-344) Another notable architect who significantly contributed to Brno's cityscape was [Arnošt Wiesner](/wiki/Arno%C5%A1t_Wiesner "Arnošt Wiesner").[[343]](#cite_note-345)[[344]](#cite_note-leisure-346)[[345]](#cite_note-komor-347) Other examples of functionalist buildings include the [Hotel Avion](/w/index.php?title=Hotel_Avion&action=edit&redlink=1 "Hotel Avion (page does not exist)") [[cs](https://cs.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hotel_Avion "cs:Hotel Avion")] and [Palace Moravia](/wiki/Palace_Moravia "Palace Moravia"). The [Brno Exhibition Centre](/wiki/Brno_Exhibition_Centre "Brno Exhibition Centre") hosts more than 40 trade fairs and conferences annually, attracting over 1 million visitors.

[Lužánky](/wiki/Lu%C5%BE%C3%A1nky "Lužánky") is the oldest public park in the Czech Republic, established in the late 18th century by [Emperor Joseph II](/wiki/Emperor_Joseph_II "Emperor Joseph II").[[346]](#cite_note-348) [Denisovy sady](/w/index.php?title=Denisovy_sady&action=edit&redlink=1 "Denisovy sady (page does not exist)") [[cs](https://cs.wikipedia.org/wiki/Denisovy_sady "cs:Denisovy sady")], founded in the early 19th century, was the first public park in the Czech Republic to be established by public authorities.[[347]](#cite_note-349) More recent additions include the [Brno astronomical clock](/wiki/Brno_astronomical_clock "Brno astronomical clock") and the [AZ Tower](/wiki/AZ_Tower "AZ Tower"), commissioned in 2013, and at 111 metres (364 ft), the tallest building in the Czech Republic.[[348]](#cite_note-350)[[349]](#cite_note-351)
